title: #announcements: 1772056123
preview:
Dev Patel: Heads up — the refreshed Support/CS escalation playbook is now published in Confluence. Please bookmark + start using this for all customer-impacting escalations.

Primary hub (start here): https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/CSS/Escalation+Playbook

What’s new (highlights):
• Escalation paths + contacts (Hosted/Dedicated/Private + when to pull SRE vs Platform): https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/CSS/Escalation+Paths+and+Contacts+2026
• Severity matrix + escalation triggers (Support P0–P4 ↔ Incident Sev0–Sev3): https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/CSS/Severity+Matrix+and+Escalation+Triggers
• SLA policy by customer tier (first response + update cadence + after-hours rules): https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/CSS/SLA+Policy+by+Customer+Tier
• Slack bridge + incident channel conventions (naming + required context + when to move to #incidents): https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/CSS/Slack+Bridge+and+Incident+Channel+Conventions
• Executive escalation protocol + summary template: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/CSS/Executive+Escalation+Protocol
• Customer comms templates (ack, mitigation updates, workaround guidance, resolution follow-up): https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/CSS/Customer+Comms+Templates

Expected adoption date: Monday 2026-02-17 (use for new SUP tickets/bridges created on/after that date; ok to keep older threads as-is).

Two asks:
1) Use the triage/intake checklist before paging (request IDs, region, model, SDK version, recent deploys): https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/CSS/Support+Triage+Intake+Checklist
2) When you open a bridge, paste the bridge template + link the SUP ticket (we’re rolling out automation to help).

Open questions / issues → reply in this thread or DM me. We’ll run 20 min office hours Tue/Thu this week to walk through changes.

(reactions: :tada: x18, :bookmark_tabs: x9, :eyes: x6)

Jasmine Liu: +1 — thanks Dev + everyone who reviewed. Please treat the update cadence language as “default policy” and loop Rachel early for any contract exceptions. :thumbsup:

Rachel Kim: Adding: for enterprise comms, please stick to the templates verbatim unless we’ve agreed otherwise in the bridge. It keeps us consistent across email + status page.

Sean Gallagher: SRE note: the severity matrix is aligned w/ paging thresholds we actually use today. If you’re debating P0 vs P1, err on the side of “page w/ complete context” vs “page early with missing details”. The required fields in the bridge template are there for a reason.

Rafael Mendes: Also if you move to #incidents, please make sure the bridge has at least 1 SUP linked + the customer impact summary (who/what/where). We lose too much time reconstructing context after the fact.

Dylan Brooks: Jira automations are in progress (INT-333). Starting next week you should see reminders for missing region/model/request IDs + an auto-prompt to link the known-issues index when relevant.

Elliot Price: Docs team will keep the “known issues” hub updated and we’ll continue to prune anything customer-specific. If you notice an article missing “when to escalate” guidance, tag @Docs in #docs and we’ll fix same day.

Owen Phillips: For Support: I’ll pin the bridge template in #support and update the internal macro library to point at these pages. If anyone hits a gap (e.g., routing/fallback confusion), send me the SUP and I’ll add it to the next KB batch.

Priya Shah: Quick q — are we standardizing the bridge channel name format now? (We’ve got a mix of `#bridge-*` and `#support-bridge-*` in the last month.)

Dev Patel: Yep — standard going forward:
• Customer escalation bridge started by Support: `#support-bridge-<customer>-<short-issue>-<mmdd>`
• If/when promoted to incident: incident bot will create the official `#inc-YYYYMMDD-<slug>` and we link back.
It’s in the Slack bridge conventions page above. :slightly_smiling_face:

Benji Okafor: Slack workflow update (INT-332) will enforce that naming format + auto-post the template + ask for SUP link + request IDs.

Sean Gallagher: Love it. Consistency helps during rotations.

(reactions: :white_check_mark: x12, :raised_hands: x7)

title: #eng-oncall: 1772045120
preview:
Rafael Mendes: want to sync on a recurring point of confusion: when does Support move an escalation from #support into #incidents vs keeping it in #support + paging oncall? seeing a few cases where we ended up with 2 parallel threads.

Dev Patel: +1. Support’s default is “start in #support, page if P0/P1”, but sometimes oncall immediately asks us to move it. would love a rule of thumb we can paste into the playbook.

Owen Phillips: from Support side: we’re trying not to spam #incidents for single-customer stuff, but we also don’t want to hide a real incident. examples we’ve struggled with: streaming resets (Acme, us-east) vs “unexpected 429 after SDK upgrade” (Aurora).

Sean Gallagher: agree we need a clean trigger. #incidents should be for anything that looks like platform degradation OR will require coordinated mitigation/comms. #support is fine for “help me debug this customer” cases.

Mina Haddad: +1. especially for routing/fallback questions—Support often needs quick confirmation of “is fallback active?” that can stay in #support if it’s just interpretation. but if fallback is activating unexpectedly at scale, that’s incident territory.

Rafael Mendes: concrete proposal:
1) start in #support for single-account triage / suspected client-side behavior
2) move to #incidents when any of the below is true:
- suspected regional/service degradation (elevated 5xx, latency, timeouts) even if only 1 customer reported so far
- multiple customers or multiple tickets reporting same symptom in short window
- needs an oncall-led mitigation (traffic shift, rollback, capacity move, config change)
- needs status page / broad comms decision
- exceeds paging threshold (aligned to SLO alerts) OR we’re going to page more than one team

Priya Shah: can we add “Private installer upgrades stuck” as an example? typically 1 customer, but requires a coordinated eng response + careful rollback steps.

Sean Gallagher: for Private: if it’s one customer blocked on upgrade, that’s usually #support + page the Private Deployments oncall (not necessarily #incidents) unless it’s a release-wide regression or we expect more customers to hit it.

Owen Phillips: makes sense. so “customer-specific but high touch” != automatically #incidents.

Dev Patel: what about embedded timeouts eu-west (like SUP-1850-style)? started as single customer, but ended up being partial degradation.

Rafael Mendes: that’s a good “start in #support but move fast” case. if the first triage checks show signals outside the customer (dashboards, error rate, synthetic), we should move to #incidents early. we lose time when we wait for “2nd customer confirmation.”

Mina Haddad: +1. also, for routing/fallback: if Support sees pinned model “ignored” (SUP-1847-type), that can stay in #support until we confirm it’s widespread. if we see unexpected fallback activations or policy violations across customers/tiers, #incidents.

Dylan Brooks: tooling angle: if we do move to #incidents, can we standardize the handoff? like: link SUP ticket(s), copy the incident template, pin request IDs + region + model. otherwise #incidents becomes scrollback archaeology.

Rafael Mendes: yes. we’re already adding “required context” for pages; same should apply for moving to #incidents.

incident-bot: Reminder: For active incidents, use /incident start in #incidents to create an incident record and status updates. (If you started in another channel, link it here.)

Owen Phillips: ok but can we be explicit about what *doesn’t* go to #incidents? we’ve had oncall ask us to move things that are basically docs / SDK troubleshooting.

Sean Gallagher: examples that should stay in #support:
- 429/quota confusion after SDK upgrade (until proven backend regression)
- structured output validation errors / schema issues
- SSO login loop for one tenant (unless auth provider outage)
- webhook SSL validation failures where it’s customer cert chain

Dev Patel: and examples that should go to #incidents:
- streaming disconnects spiking in a region (even if only 1 enterprise customer notices first)
- rerank endpoint 500s on large batches if we see elevated 5xx
- “console dashboards missing regions” only if telemetry pipeline is broken for multiple customers

Mina Haddad: one nuance: #incidents isn’t the same as “page SRE”. we can have an incident w/ Platform owning. but #incidents is still the right place when we need one shared source of truth + comms.

Rafael Mendes: exactly. #incidents is coordination + decision log. paging is separate.

Priya Shah: what about update cadence? Support sometimes keeps it in #support because they’re doing customer updates and don’t want to overcomplicate.

Sean Gallagher: if it’s in #incidents, it actually helps. we can align: who is customer-facing writer, what’s approved to say, and when to post status page vs docs banner.

Dev Patel: ok I’m sold. can we write the “move to #incidents” criteria in one pasteable block?

Rafael Mendes: draft pasteable guidance (feel free to edit):
“Keep in #support when: it’s a single-customer issue with no evidence of platform degradation; suspected client config/SDK usage; needs targeted debugging.
Move to #incidents when: suspected service/regional degradation; multiple customers affected; mitigation requires coordinated eng action (rollback/traffic shift/capacity); we may post a status update or need cross-team coordination. When moving: link all SUP tickets, paste incident context (customer/tier, region, model, request IDs, start time, impact), and pin it.”

Owen Phillips: :thumbsup: add one line: “Don’t wait for 2nd customer if dashboards show it.”

Rafael Mendes: +1. adding.

Dylan Brooks: I’ll align Slack workflow with this. INT-332/INT-333 already tracking the automation; we can add a step that prompts “Move to #incidents? (Y/N)” w/ the criteria + link to template.

Dev Patel: I’ll update the escalation playbook section + share in #support. also will add the examples list (429 vs streaming vs private upgrade).

Sean Gallagher: please include the note that #incidents is not a backdoor to bypass incident process. once it’s there, we should use /incident if it’s truly incident-worthy; otherwise keep it in #support.

Mina Haddad: and for routing-specific escalations, add “if fallback is active for a single customer, confirm in #support; if fallback activation is unexpected or widespread, move to #incidents.”

Rafael Mendes: cool. I’ll post a summary and we’ll treat this as the default.

Rafael Mendes: SUMMARY / agreement:
- Default start: #support for triage.
- Move to #incidents when there’s evidence/suspicion of platform or regional degradation, multi-customer impact, need for coordinated mitigation/comms, or cross-team paging.
- Don’t wait for a second report if dashboards/synthetics indicate an issue.
- Private “single customer blocked” stays in #support unless it’s a release-wide regression.
- When moving to #incidents: link SUP tickets + paste/pin context (region/model/request IDs/impact) and use /incident when appropriate.

Dev Patel: :thumbsup: will incorporate verbatim.

title: #docs: 1772040999
preview:
Elliot Price: Quick review pass on the workaround articles before we publish batch 1. Main theme: we need a *much* clearer “When to escalate” section + remove anything customer-identifying. Can folks skim and drop notes here?

Wei Chen: +1. I went through streaming timeouts + 429 quota mismatch. Both are solid technically, but the escalation guidance is kind of buried.
Wei Chen: Suggest we standardize a block at the end:
- When to escalate to Support
- What to include (request IDs, region, model, SDK version)
- What *not* to send (full prompts / PII)

Kira Thompson: I reviewed Dedicated autoscaling + Private installer upgrade. Private draft has a few lines that look like they came straight from a customer ticket (cluster name + exact timestamps). We should generalize those.

Dev Patel: Agree. Please treat these as “Support-safe / customer-facing” by default. If we need internal-only details, put them in the SUP ticket or the internal runbook, not the KB.

Priya Shah: On the “When to escalate” language: do we want hard thresholds or softer guidance? Like “if still failing after 3 retries” vs “if error rate >2%”?

Sean Gallagher: For public KB, avoid our internal paging thresholds. Use customer-observable signals.
Sean Gallagher: Example wording that works:
- “If the issue persists for >15 minutes despite the mitigations below”
- “If multiple regions are affected”
- “If you’re on Dedicated/Private and impact is production-blocking”
Sean Gallagher: And then Support can map that to P0/P1 internally.

Naomi Feldman: Also please scrub anything that could be considered sensitive operational detail:
- specific hostnames/IPs
- screenshots with account names
- copy/pasted logs that include tokens, emails, org IDs
Naomi Feldman: It’s fine to show *redacted* examples (e.g., `req_...`), but make the redaction explicit.

Elliot Price: Great. I’ll add a standard section template to each article:
1) Summary
2) Symptoms
3) Affected tiers/regions/models
4) Workarounds (step-by-step)
5) Verification (how to confirm it helped)
6) When to escalate
7) What to include in your Support request

Wei Chen: Streaming timeouts article: can we make the escalation callout more direct? Right now it says “contact Support if you need help” — too vague.
Wei Chen: Proposed:
“Escalate if: (a) streaming resets continue after setting a 60s idle timeout + retry with backoff, (b) you see consistent disconnects on a single region, or (c) you have request IDs showing server-side `stream_reset` errors.”

Dev Patel: +1, but remove “`stream_reset`” as a named internal classification unless customers already see it. Better: “errors indicating connection reset / incomplete chunked transfer” etc.

Kira Thompson: Dedicated autoscaling article: it mentions one customer’s exact config values (min=2, cooldown=90s) and calls it “Northstar recommended.” That has to go.
Kira Thompson: We can rewrite as ranges + explanation:
- “Start with min replicas = typical steady-state load (often 2–4)”
- “Set scale-down cooldown to at least 5–10 minutes if you see oscillation”
- “Escalate if latency SLOs are breached or scaling events >N per hour” (but phrase it customer-observable: “scaling events continuously for >30 minutes”).

Sean Gallagher: Yep. Keep it “if you see repeated scale up/down events every few minutes for >30m” rather than “>N/hour.”

Priya Shah: 429 / quota mismatch draft: it includes a snippet of a customer’s header values with their org ID embedded. I’ll replace with an anonymized example.

Naomi Feldman: Thank you. Also: avoid telling customers to disable security controls (e.g., “turn off TLS verification”). If there’s a debugging step like that, label it “DO NOT use in production” and ideally omit from KB.

Wei Chen: For the 429 article, we should add “Common misconfig” section:
- multiple concurrent retries causing burst
- not respecting `Retry-After`
- mixing keys across envs

Dev Patel: And include “What Support will ask for” so customers can pre-empt it:
- time window
- region
- endpoint
- request IDs
- SDK version
- whether they’re behind a proxy

Elliot Price: Action items I’m taking from this thread:
- Add a consistent “When to escalate” section to each article
- Remove customer names / configs / org IDs; only anonymized examples
- Align escalation language with customer-observable signals (no internal paging thresholds)
- Add “What to include” + safe log collection guidance

Kira Thompson: I’ll do another sweep specifically for Private upgrade article for any customer artifacts + make sure rollback guidance is framed as “contact Support before attempting rollback” for regulated customers.

Sean Gallagher: If you want one line that helps Support triage faster, add: “Include installer logs + timestamps” but don’t paste huge logs inline; attach via secure channel / Support portal.

Naomi Feldman: +1. Say “we may request logs via a secure upload link” so we’re not encouraging email attachments.

Wei Chen: ETA for updated drafts? I’m blocked on final copy for the SDK troubleshooting cross-link until these stabilize.

Elliot Price: I’ll push edits today and tag you both for a final sanity check. Goal is publish by tomorrow EOD so we can link from Support macros.

Dev Patel: Appreciate it. Once the “When to escalate” is consistent, Support can copy/paste without rewriting every time.

Elliot Price: Cool. I’ll also update the publishing checklist to include “customer-specific detail scrub” as a hard gate.

“Escalate if: (a) streaming resets continue after setting a 60s idle timeout + retry with backoff, (b) you see consistent disconnects on a single region, or (c) you have request IDs showing server-side `stream_reset` errors.”

Dev Patel: +1, but remove “`stream_reset`” as a named internal classification unless customers already see it. Better: “errors indicating connection reset / incomplete chunked transfer” etc.

Kira Thompson: Dedicated autoscaling article: it mentions one customer’s exact config values (min=2, cooldown=90s) and calls it “Northstar recommended.” That has to go.
Kira Thompson: We can rewrite as ranges + explanation:
- “Start with min replicas = typical steady-state load (often 2–4)”
- “Set scale-down cooldown to at least 5–10 minutes if you see oscillation”
- “Escalate if latency SLOs are breached or scaling events >N per hour” (but phrase it customer-observable: “scaling events continuously for >30 minutes”).

Sean Gallagher: Yep. Keep it “if you see repeated scale up/down events every few minutes for >30m” rather than “>N/hour.”

Priya Shah: 429 / quota mismatch draft: it includes a snippet of a customer’s header values with their org ID embedded. I’ll replace with an anonymized example.

Naomi Feldman: Thank you. Also: avoid telling customers to disable security controls (e.g., “turn off TLS verification”). If there’s a debugging step like that, label it “DO NOT use in production” and ideally omit from KB.

Wei Chen: For the 429 article, we should add “Common misconfig” section:
- multiple concurrent retries causing burst
- not respecting `Retry-After`
- mixing keys across envs

Dev Patel: And include “What Support will ask for” so customers can pre-empt it:
- time window
- region
- endpoint
- request IDs
- SDK version
- whether they’re behind a proxy

Elliot Price: Action items I’m taking from this thread:
- Add a consistent “When to escalate” section to each article
- Remove customer names / configs / org IDs; only anonymized examples
- Align escalation language with customer-observable signals (no internal paging thresholds)
- Add “What to include” + safe log collection guidance

Kira Thompson: I’ll do another sweep specifically for Private upgrade article for any customer artifacts + make sure rollback guidance is framed as “contact Support before attempting rollback” for regulated customers.

Sean Gallagher: If you want one line that helps Support triage faster, add: “Include installer logs + timestamps” but don’t paste huge logs inline; attach via secure channel / Support portal.

Naomi Feldman: +1. Say “we may request logs via a secure upload link” so we’re not encouraging email attachments.

Wei Chen: ETA for updated drafts? I’m blocked on final copy for the SDK troubleshooting cross-link until these stabilize.

Elliot Price: I’ll push edits today and tag you both for a final sanity check. Goal is publish by tomorrow EOD so we can link from Support macros.

Dev Patel: Appreciate it. Once the “When to escalate” is consistent, Support can copy/paste without rewriting every time.

Elliot Price: Cool. I’ll also update the publishing checklist to include “customer-specific detail scrub” as a hard gate.

title: #eng-oncall: 1772003901
preview:
Rafael Mendes: hey folks — quick ask from on-call side. we’re getting paged from Support with partial context and it slows triage a lot. can we standardize what goes into a page / escalation message? ideally a pinned template Support can copy/paste into bridges + PD notes.

Owen Phillips: +1. we’ve been trying to push “request IDs + region” but it’s not consistently included. what’s your must-have list?

Rafael Mendes: must-have for any page (Hosted/Dedicated/Private):
- customer/tier (self-serve/dedicated/private/enterprise)
- impact summary (what broke, % failures, latency, etc.)
- *request IDs* (>=3) + approx time window (with timezone)
- region + endpoint (chat/embeddings/rerank/etc.)
- model + version (and whether pinned)
- error snippets (sanitized) + HTTP status codes
- what’s already been tried (retries, model swap, region swap, etc.)
- any “recent changes” signal (deploy in last 2h / runtime version change / config rollout)
- links: SUP ticket + bridge thread

Dev Patel: makes sense. for “recent changes” can Support reliably know deploys? we can add “not sure / please confirm” but don’t want to block paging.

Rafael Mendes: totally fine if unknown. even “we don’t know if there was a deploy” is useful vs silence. if you can include “customer noticed starting ~30 mins ago” that helps correlate.

Priya Shah: can you drop the exact template you want? we can pin in #support and also bake into the Slack bridge workflow (INT-332).

Rafael Mendes: yep — proposed copy/paste:
```
[PAGING CONTEXT] (P0/P1) <short summary>
Customer: <name> | Tier: <hosted/dedicated/private/enterprise>
Scope: <# users/tenants affected> | Regions: <us-east/eu-west/...>
Endpoint: <chat/completions/embeddings/rerank> | SDK: <py/ts/go + version>
Model: <name> | Version/pinned?: <yes/no + version>
Impact: <error rate %, latency p95 delta, “streams reset mid-gen”, etc.>
Time window: <start-end> <timezone>
Request IDs (3+):
- req_...
- req_...
- req_...
Errors (sanitized):
- <status code + message>
What we tried / current mitigation:
- <retry/backoff, region failover, disable http2, etc.>
Recent changes?: <unknown / runtime X.Y / config rollout link>
Links:
- Jira SUP-####: <link>
- Slack thread/bridge: <link>
- Dashboards (if any): <link>
```
key thing: request IDs + model/version + region. those three are the difference between “we can act” and “we have to ask 5 questions”.

Sean Gallagher: +1 from SRE. also helps us decide quickly whether this is a “move to #incidents” situation vs keep in support bridge.

Owen Phillips: looks good. question: do you want “severity” too or just Support priority?

Rafael Mendes: include Support priority (P0–P4). if you also know suspected Sev (Sev0–Sev3) that’s fine, but don’t bikeshed. we can map on our side.

Priya Shah: for request IDs — sometimes customers only give timestamps + correlation id in their app logs. ok to page without IDs?

Rafael Mendes: if it’s truly impossible, page with:
- customer’s IP ranges (if relevant)
- their org_id / project_id (internal) if Support can pull it
- exact timestamps + region + endpoint
but please label “NO REQUEST IDs AVAILABLE” so we know to route to log search differently.

Mina Haddad: can we add one line for “fallback/routing involved?” we’ve seen confusion on pinned versions vs fallback.

Rafael Mendes: good call. add:
“Routing/fallback: <unknown/observed> | customer sees pinned version?: <yes/no>”
(and if you have it, link the routing/fallback dashboard).

Dev Patel: action items:
1) we’ll pin this template in #support + #customer-success
2) add to escalation playbook page + bridge workflow prompt (INT-332)
3) update Jira intake checklist to require: region, model, request IDs, time window

Owen Phillips: :thumbsup: i’ll also nudge the team to include at least 3 request IDs every time. we’ll add a macro snippet.

Sean Gallagher: thanks. if you want a hard rule: any P0/P1 page missing request IDs gets bounced back w/ “need IDs to proceed” unless it’s clearly a broad outage.

Rafael Mendes: +1. broad outage exception: if multiple customers + elevated error rates, page anyway and we’ll correlate via metrics.

Priya Shah: cool. i’ll draft the pinned message + send for quick review here before posting.

Rafael Mendes: sounds good. :pray: this will save us a ton of cycles on the next streaming incident.

title: #announcements: 1771234802
preview:
Marissa Cole: :tada: Auto-rollback on SLO breach (MVP) is now available for INTERNAL opt-in (behind a feature flag).

What it does (MVP):
• During canary/A-B/staged rollouts, we evaluate latency + error-rate SLO burn-rate for the rollout scope (route + model version + region + tier).
• If we detect a breach per policy, controller will auto-*pause* the rollout (default) or auto-*rollback* (if enabled in policy).
• Every decision is recorded in the rollout audit trail + appears in Console.

What it does NOT do (yet):
• Multi-region failover orchestration
• Quality/eval-based rollback signals

Docs / links:
• PRD (internal): https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/PROD/Auto-rollback+on+SLO+breach+integration
• Runbook (oncall + service owners): https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ENGSRE/Auto-rollback+on+SLO+breach+runbook
• Metrics naming + labels: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ARCH/Rollouts+SLO+metrics+naming+and+labeling
• Default thresholds + templates (PM draft): https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/redwood/console-rollouts/auto-rollback-policy-options
• Dashboards/alerts PR (Grafana pack): https://github.com/observability-pack/pull/913

How to opt in (staging first; prod after SRE sign-off):
1) Confirm you have SLO monitors in slo-toolkit for:
   • p95 latency (fast+slow burn)
   • error rate (5xx)
   Monitors must be scoped to your rollout dimensions (route/model version/region). If you’re missing coverage, reply here and we’ll route to owners.
2) Enable the feature flag for your service:
   • `rollouts.auto_rollback.enabled=true` (service-level)
   • (Optional) `rollouts.auto_rollback.allow_rollback=true` (if you want rollback vs pause)
3) Add policy section to your rollout config (example below) OR use a template in Console Policy Editor (new section: “Auto-rollback (SLO)”).

Policy snippet (example):
```json
{
  "autoRollback": {
    "enabled": true,
    "action": "PAUSE",
    "signals": ["LATENCY_P95", "ERROR_RATE"],
    "windows": {"fast": "5m", "slow": "30m"},
    "debounce": "10m",
    "minRequests": 2000,
    "suppressDuringIncident": true
  }
}
```

Guardrails worth calling out:
• Minimum volume gate (avoids false positives on low traffic)
• Dual-window confirmation (fast+slow burn)
• Debounce/holdoff to prevent oscillation
• If monitors are stale/missing, controller will NOT rollback; it will log `monitor_missing_or_stale` and keep rollout in current state (and we’ll alert).

If you want to opt in, reply with:
• service + route
• model(s) + region(s)
• tier (hosted/dedicated/private)
• desired action (PAUSE vs ROLLBACK)
• owner + oncall channel

Owners for questions:
• Product/policy: @Paula Mendes
• Rollout controller: @Jada Williams / @Chloe Martin
• SLO semantics + oncall readiness: @Sean Gallagher / @Rafael Mendes

Thank you—this is a big reliability milestone.

Paula Mendes: Adding: Console UI is behind the same flag. If you don’t see the “Auto-rollback (SLO)” section in Policy Editor, ping me + include your org/service.

Jada Williams: Small clarification: rollback action currently uses a step-down strategy for traffic split (rate-limited) + is idempotent, but we’re still staging it per-service. If you’re the first prod adopter, expect tighter coordination.

Sean Gallagher: SRE note: for prod enablement we require (a) runbook link in your service doc, (b) dashboard access for oncall, (c) monitors verified non-stale for 7 days. We’ll do a quick 15m sign-off.

Aisha Bello: Dashboards are landing today; alerting is notify-only by default (no pages) until we tune thresholds. If your service opts in, we’ll add routing to the right Slack channel.

Connor O'Brien: Release note: feature flag defaults OFF in prod. Staging enablement is self-serve; prod enablement goes through ENG-4850 checklist. If you need help flipping flags, open a request in #eng-releases.

deploy-bot: Observability pack updated in staging: `observability-pack@v2.31.0` (includes rollout auto-rollback dashboards)

Felix Schneider: Audit trail: Console shows an “Auto action” event with decision outcome + suppression reason + links to monitor defs. If you’re missing links/fields, file a bug + include rollout_id.

Rafael Mendes: We’re running a game day next week (latency spike, 5xx burst, stale monitor). If your team wants to participate as a pilot adopter, reply and we’ll slot you in.

Marissa Cole: Thanks all. Pilot target: 2–3 internal services in staging this week, then 1 internal prod route next week. We’ll post follow-ups here with known issues / tuning changes.

Ethan Park: FYI: policy validation will reject unsafe configs (e.g., debounce < 5m, minRequests < 500). Error messages should point to the exact field.

Allison Grant: Please do NOT add free-form labels to the new metrics. Stick to the standard label set in the doc (cardinality limits). If you need an extra dimension, ask in #eng-sre first.

incident-bot: Reminder: auto-rollback events will post to Slack once incident-bot PR-477 is deployed to prod. Current staging notifications go to #eng-sre-test.

Paula Mendes: FAQ I’m getting in DMs: yes, you can set per-rollout overrides (e.g., stricter for a risky model version) and keep service defaults otherwise. UI will show both + which one won.

Chloe Martin: One more note: if controller can’t fetch SLO status (toolkit timeout), it will treat as “no signal” and log it. We’re tracking rates of this; please report if you see it frequently.

Marissa Cole: If you’re replying to opt in, please include your preferred window for enablement (this week vs next) so we can manage support load.

title: #architecture: 1770219804
preview:
Noah Patel: Dropping ADR here for review: short/long prompt-aware scheduling policy (two-queue + aging) for mixed workloads. Main goal: cut p95 tail without tanking throughput. Please focus on fairness + multi-tenant implications + gating requirements. Link: confluence://architecture-and-standards/decision-records/adr-short-long-prompt-scheduling-policy

Lena Fischer: tl;dr: classify reqs as short/long based on prompt tokens + predicted prefill cost, keep two queues, dispatch biased toward short w/ token budget + aging so long makes progress. Feature-flagged, defaults off.

Architecture Bot: ADR review opened by Noah Patel. Requested reviewers: @architecture-core @eng-sre

Sofia Ivanova: For context: this is prototype phase only. Not trying to productize tenant QoS yet, but we need to not accidentally create one (or create unfairness that trips up Dedicated customers).

Sean Gallagher: SRE hat: I’m mostly worried about “silent policy change” in multi-tenant hosted pools. If we bias shorts, long-context tenants could see degraded p95/p99 and we won’t notice unless we split metrics by class + tenant. Needs strong guardrails.

Noah Patel: +1. ADR currently says “fairness guardrails” but I want it explicitly defined. What’s the fairness objective? Token fairness? Request fairness? Time-based? Right now it reads like: “try to keep shorts fast, ensure longs don’t starve” which is… not a policy, it’s a hope :slightly_smiling_face:

Lena Fischer: Fair call. For prototype we’re optimizing user-visible latency (TTFT/p95) for “short” while guaranteeing bounded waiting for long via aging. We’re not guaranteeing strict per-tenant fairness. We can add explicit invariants:
- starvation bound: max time-in-queue for long < X (config)
- dispatch share floor for long when long queue depth > 0
- cap on consecutive short dispatch iterations

Hiro Tanaka: Kernel side question: does class selection affect kernel auto-selection? We’ve had issues where mixing seq lengths causes kernel thrash. If policy oscillates between short/long every iteration, we could make that worse.

Lena Fischer: We’re planning token budgeting per “scheduler iteration” so we can batch multiple shorts before switching. Also config “min_run_length” for a class to avoid oscillation. But yes, kernel selection stability is a risk.

Noah Patel: Multi-tenant: even if not “QoS product”, if hosted pool has two classes, we’re implicitly prioritizing one traffic type over another. If a single tenant floods shorts, they can starve others’ longs unless aging is global not per-tenant. How are we defining aging scope?

Sofia Ivanova: Current implementation in PR draft is global per-pool queues. Aging is per-request based on enqueue time, not per-tenant. That helps starvation but still means a tenant with lots of shorts increases contention overall.

Paula Mendes: Product/rollout perspective: we *need* this gated per pool + per model, and ideally per customer tier in Dedicated. We also need a comms plan if Dedicated customers see long-context slowdowns.

Sean Gallagher: Gating requirements (IMO non-negotiable):
1) feature flag default OFF
2) allowlist pools (canary only)
3) runtime config bounds (no “set bias=1000 and melt GPU”)
4) metrics split by class + overall (p50/p95/p99, queue time, TTFT)
5) rollback is “flip flag” + revert params, no deploy required

Allison Grant: On metrics: we should align with existing naming conventions. Please don’t ship ad-hoc labels that explode cardinality. If you want tenant visibility, we need to do it via sampled traces or aggregation by “pool/model/class”, not “customer_id”.

Noah Patel: +1 on cardinality. ADR should explicitly say “no per-tenant labels”. But we still need to reason about tenant fairness without measuring it directly. Maybe define success criteria at pool level and watch for regressions in long-class p95.

Lena Fischer: Agree. Proposed instrumentation:
- scheduler_queue_depth{class=short|long}
- scheduler_time_in_queue_ms_bucket{class=...}
- scheduler_dispatch_share{class=...}
- scheduler_starvation_events_total{class=long}
- ttft_ms{class=...} (already exists, we add class label w/ bounded cardinality)

Sean Gallagher: Make sure “class” label is strictly {short,long} (and maybe “unknown”) and not a freeform tier.

Hiro Tanaka: Also please gate “prefill/decode separation hooks” behind the same flag. We don’t want the refactor to change behavior when policy is off.

Sofia Ivanova: Yes. Plan is: with flag off, code path should behave exactly like today’s queue selection. We’ll add regression tests around batch composition and ordering.

Noah Patel: Another concern: this looks like SRPT-lite. In multi-tenant systems SRPT can be unfair under certain distributions. ADR should include explicit statement: “We’re not implementing SRPT; we’re implementing two-class scheduling with bounded starvation.” And list failure modes.

Lena Fischer: Will update ADR with a section:
- Not SRPT (no continuous reordering by remaining tokens)

Noah Patel: Dropping ADR here for review: short/long prompt-aware scheduling policy (two-queue + aging) for mixed workloads. Main goal: cut p95 tail without tanking throughput. Please focus on fairness + multi-tenant implications + gating requirements. Link: confluence://architecture-and-standards/decision-records/adr-short-long-prompt-scheduling-policy

Lena Fischer: tl;dr: classify reqs as short/long based on prompt tokens + predicted prefill cost, keep two queues, dispatch biased toward short w/ token budget + aging so long makes progress. Feature-flagged, defaults off.

Architecture Bot: ADR review opened by Noah Patel. Requested reviewers: @architecture-core @eng-sre

Sofia Ivanova: For context: this is prototype phase only. Not trying to productize tenant QoS yet, but we need to not accidentally create one (or create unfairness that trips up Dedicated customers).

Sean Gallagher: SRE hat: I’m mostly worried about “silent policy change” in multi-tenant hosted pools. If we bias shorts, long-context tenants could see degraded p95/p99 and we won’t notice unless we split metrics by class + tenant. Needs strong guardrails.

Noah Patel: +1. ADR currently says “fairness guardrails” but I want it explicitly defined. What’s the fairness objective? Token fairness? Request fairness? Time-based? Right now it reads like: “try to keep shorts fast, ensure longs don’t starve” which is… not a policy, it’s a hope :slightly_smiling_face:

Lena Fischer: Fair call. For prototype we’re optimizing user-visible latency (TTFT/p95) for “short” while guaranteeing bounded waiting for long via aging. We’re not guaranteeing strict per-tenant fairness. We can add explicit invariants:
- starvation bound: max time-in-queue for long < X (config)
- dispatch share floor for long when long queue depth > 0
- cap on consecutive short dispatch iterations

Hiro Tanaka: Kernel side question: does class selection affect kernel auto-selection? We’ve had issues where mixing seq lengths causes kernel thrash. If policy oscillates between short/long every iteration, we could make that worse.

Lena Fischer: We’re planning token budgeting per “scheduler iteration” so we can batch multiple shorts before switching. Also config “min_run_length” for a class to avoid oscillation. But yes, kernel selection stability is a risk.

Sofia Ivanova: Current implementation in PR draft is global per-pool queues. Aging is per-request based on enqueue time, not per-tenant. That helps starvation but still means a tenant with lots of shorts increases contention overall.

preview:
Incident Bot: :rotating_light: Paging alert: perf-canary regression storm detected (dedupe disabled due to unique labels)

- Signal: `perf_canary_p95_latency_ms` + `perf_canary_tokens_per_sec`
- Affected: 27 alert instances across models/regions/SKU in last 5m
- Example: `model=llama-3-70b-instruct` `region=us-east` `sku=h100-80g` p95 +18% vs baseline (n=62)
- Runbook: https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/SRE/Runbook%3A+Perf+Regression+Canary+Alert
- Dashboard: https://grafana.redwood.ai/d/perf-canary/perf-canary?var-region=us-east

Thread created for incident coordination. Please acknowledge.

Rafael Mendes: ack. seeing my pager light up like a christmas tree. is this real or canary gone wild?

Sean Gallagher: ack. looking at incidents channel, multiple pages within 2 mins. treating as alerting failure until proven otherwise.

Noah Patel: ack. I’m on. pulling canary logs + baseline store status.

Grafana: Alert state change: 11 firing -> 19 firing (rule: perf-canary-p95-regression-page)

Allison Grant: ack. can someone confirm whether this is *paging* rules only or also warnings? (did we just flip paging in more dims?)

Rafael Mendes: looks like paging rules. warnings also firing but those go to #eng-runtime normally.

Sofia Ivanova: ack. checking scheduler metrics on one of the “regressed” models. initial glance: queueing time flat-ish, but query shows lots of new label combos.

Logan Wright: ack. I’m suspicious of cardinality change. we had a schema review earlier this week.

Nadia Rahman: ack. baseline job ran ~30m ago (I see artifact upload). might’ve rolled baseline version?

Omar El-Sayed: ack. checking runtime tag emission; we added kernel tags recently. could be exploding labelset.

Vanessa Ortiz: I can check if any fleet change happened last hour (sku reshuffle / driver). but the simultaneity across regions smells config/telemetry.

Rafael Mendes: immediate goal: stop paging noise. can we disable paging rules or canary eval while we triage?

Sean Gallagher: +1. per runbook we can:
1) set incident-bot dedupe key override
2) disable paging rules in observability-pack
3) lower canary QPS to near-zero if needed

Noah Patel: I can flip canary `capacity_budget_pct` down to 0.05% via control plane if needed, but pages are already happening due to alerts on existing metrics.

Allison Grant: let’s first mute paging rule globally for 60m. Rafael can you do silence?

Rafael Mendes: doing silence now in Alertmanager for `perf-canary-*page*` rules. ETA 2 min.

Grafana: Alert silenced by Rafael Mendes (duration: 1h, matcher: alertname=~"perf-canary-.*page.*")

Rafael Mendes: silence in place. still need root cause + prevent recurrence.

Noah Patel: pulled one alert payload from incident-bot. it includes `runtime_build_id` and `kernel_id` labels now.

```
labels:
  model: llama-3-70b-instruct
  model_version: 3.1.2
  region: us-east
  tier: hosted
  sku: h100-80g
  runtime_build_id: runtime-2026.02.02-rc3
  kernel_id: flashattn_v3_sm90_seq128k_batchdyn
  kernel_family: flashattn_v3
  prompt_bucket: p90
  canary_profile: default
```

That’s… a lot.

Logan Wright: yeah that’s the issue. if alert grouping includes kernel_id + prompt_bucket, you’ll get 100s of unique series, each with small n, baseline mismatch, and dedupe breaks.

Sofia Ivanova: also prompt_bucket being a label is super risky. any drift in distribution -> new time series -> baseline “missing” -> evaluator might be falling back to something dumb.

Nadia Rahman: confirming: baseline store key is `model + model_version + sku (+region optional)`.
If canary emits metrics with extra labels but baseline lookup doesn’t strip them, you get “baseline not found” -> and evaluator could treat baseline=0 or default baseline.

Noah Patel: digging in canary evaluator code: baseline fetch uses the full labelset for keying unless `baseline_key_labels` config is set. I think it’s currently unset in prod. :facepalm:

Allison Grant: ok so this is config regression. why did it surface now?

Omar El-Sayed: runtime PR added `kernel_id` tag to telemetry last week; canary started emitting it yesterday in us-east; then today we enabled paging rules with “top models” and it blew up.

Caleb Johnson: also: ingestion delay in telemetry right now (prom remote-write lag ~3-4m). that can affect “min samples” logic if it’s based on wall clock.

Vanessa Ortiz: fleet: no major change. driver update is scheduled tomorrow. nothing that would instantly hit all regions.

Rafael Mendes: action items right now:
1) fix the alert grouping / label set
2) fix canary baseline lookup to ignore high-card labels
3) re-enable paging when stable

Noah Patel: I can hotfix canary config to set `baseline_key_labels=[model,model_version,region,sku,tier]` and also set metrics emission allowlist.
But that’s a deploy.

Connor O'Brien: if you want, I can coordinate with releases to fast-track canary config change (control plane) vs full deploy.
What is adjustable without redeploy?

Noah Patel: baseline_key_labels is config in control plane (no code change). metric labels are code-side; but we can disable extra labels by setting `emit_kernel_labels=false` in canary config (also control plane).

Logan Wright: for long-term: telemetry should still carry kernel_id, but alerts shouldn’t group on it. dashboards can show it as a breakdown via exemplars or logs.

Allison Grant: yes. policy: paging alerts must not include high-cardinality labels in the alert identity. at most model/version/region/tier.

Sean Gallagher: +1. and `prompt_bucket` should never be in paging series. it’s a dimension for analysis, not alerting.

Noah Patel: making config changes now:
- `emit_kernel_labels=false`
- `emit_prompt_bucket_label=false`
- `baseline_key_labels=model,model_version,region,sku,tier`
- increase `min_samples_paging` from 50 -> 200 (temporary)

Will apply to us-east first.

Connor O'Brien: ok. once applied, we should watch warning rules (not silenced) to see if they settle.

Grafana: Alert state change: 19 firing -> 6 firing (rule: perf-canary-p95-regression-warn)

Sofia Ivanova: I’m seeing the number of series drop in Grafana. good sign. queueing/decode lat look normal-ish now.

Nadia Rahman: baseline fetch logs from canary before:
`baseline_lookup_miss rate=0.78`
Now after config change:
`baseline_lookup_miss rate=0.03`

That was the storm.

Rafael Mendes: can we keep paging silenced until end of day and then re-enable after we confirm stability for a couple hours?

Sean Gallagher: yes. also: we need a writeup. this is essentially an incident caused by alerting/config, not perf regression.

Allison Grant: agreed. Sev? I’d call it Sev3 internal (oncall disruption) with follow-ups.

Rafael Mendes: logging as internal incident, Sev3. primary impact: oncall noise + risk of missing real pages.

Logan Wright: we should also add a guardrail: metrics-cardinality audit in CI for canary changes.

Caleb Johnson: and add an alert on cardinality itself. like “number of active series for perf_canary_* > threshold”.

Omar El-Sayed: +1. plus explicit “do not include in baseline key” list for labels: `kernel_id`, `prompt_bucket`, `request_id` (lol), etc.

Noah Patel: I’ll open a follow-up ticket to make baseline lookup *always* use explicit key labels with safe defaults. right now it’s too footgun-y.

Vanessa Ortiz: any chance this impacted customer traffic? canary overhead maybe spiked due to fanout?

Noah Patel: canary QPS stayed under cap; overhead ~0.3% per region. no evidence of inference impact. this was alerting noise, not load.

Sean Gallagher: ok. next steps in-thread:
- keep paging silenced 1h
- confirm series count stable
- write postmortem + update alert policy doc

Allison Grant: I’ll update the alerting policy doc: “paging series identity must be <= (model, model_version, region, tier)” and require min n.

Rafael Mendes: I’ll add a note to oncall training + runbook: if you see many pages with unique label combos, check label explosion + silence paging rule.

Noah Patel: update: rolled config to eu-west + us-west as well. baseline miss rate now low across regions.

Grafana: Alert state change: 6 firing -> 1 firing (rule: perf-canary-p95-regression-warn)

Sofia Ivanova: remaining warning is llama-3-8b in eu-west with p95 +9%. likely real-ish but warning-level only. can follow up later.

Nadia Rahman: baseline drift is another issue: baseline artifacts updated earlier today with different sample window (traffic mix changed). we should talk about rolling vs fixed baseline in ADR.

Logan Wright: yep. but this incident was more “baseline key mismatch” than drift. still, drift makes it worse and increases fire rate.

Sean Gallagher: can someone link the baseline drift thread? I remember we discussed anti-flap + smoothing.

Nadia Rahman: thread: https://slack.redwood.ai/archives/eng-runtime/p1768604412-baseline-drift

Allison Grant: we should capture both in postmortem:
- primary: label/cardinality + baseline-key mismatch
- contributing: baseline update + ingestion lag
- mitigation: silence paging, reduce labels, set baseline_key_labels
- prevention: guardrails + ADR + templates

Rafael Mendes: I’ll create postmortem doc and assign owners. Noah can you be DRI for tech fixes? Allison for policy? Logan/Nadia for telemetry/baseline sections.

Noah Patel: yep.

Logan Wright: yes.

Nadia Rahman: yes.

Allison Grant: yes.

Incident Bot: Status update requested. Please provide current status and next update ETA.

Rafael Mendes: Status: paging silenced, storm stopped after canary config change to remove high-card labels + correct baseline key. Monitoring warnings for 2h. Next update in 60m; plan to re-enable paging tomorrow after rule/template changes.

Connor O'Brien: note for releases: we should block any further paging enablement until the alert rule grouping is updated in observability-pack. I’ll coordinate.

Sean Gallagher: ok closing the loop: once postmortem is drafted, we’ll un-silence paging in a controlled window with one model/region.

Rafael Mendes: +1. leaving thread open until postmortem link is live.

Allison Grant: quick PR idea: in alert rule, add `sum by (model, model_version, region, tier)` *before* evaluating regression. even if extra labels exist, they won’t affect alert identity.

Caleb Johnson: that would help a lot. and maybe enforce via recording rule.

Noah Patel: agreed. ok I’m off to implement guardrail + open ENG tickets.

Rafael Mendes: ok. I’ll post postmortem link here once created.

Rafael Mendes: Postmortem doc stub: https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/ONCALL/Postmortem%3A+Perf+Canary+False+Positive+Alert+Storm (please add details + action items)

Incident Bot: :white_check_mark: Incident marked mitigated by Rafael Mendes (Sev3). Reminder: add follow-ups in Linear and link here when filed.

Rafael Mendes: I’ll create postmortem doc and assign owners. Noah can you be DRI for tech fixes? Allison for policy? Logan/Nadia for telemetry/baseline sections.

Noah Patel: yep.

Logan Wright: yes.

Nadia Rahman: yes.

Allison Grant: yes.

Sean Gallagher: ok closing the loop: once postmortem is drafted, we’ll un-silence paging in a controlled window with one model/region.

Rafael Mendes: +1. leaving thread open until postmortem link is live.

title: #eng-sre: 1770039055
preview:
Sean Gallagher: Trying to unblock defaults for auto-rollback MVP. We need sane thresholds + windows that won’t flap us into constant pause/rollback. Can folks weigh in on burn-rate patterns we’ve used successfully? Specifically p95/p99 latency + 5xx error rate.

Aisha Bello: +1. From alerting side we can support multi-window burn rate like (5m, 30m) fast/slow, and optionally (1h, 6h) for longer SLOs. But the controller needs a deterministic rule set (and min sample size) or we’ll get rollback storms.

Rafael Mendes: Historical data point: the “reranker v3” incident last fall. Traffic ramped from 5% to 25%, p99 popped for ~7 minutes (GC + cache warm), then settled. If we had a naive “p99 > threshold for 5m => rollback” it would have rolled back unnecessarily.

Rafael Mendes: Another: dedicated customer in us-east, transient 502 burst due to upstream auth. Would have triggered error-rate rollback for model rollout even though the model wasn’t the cause. So we need some guardrails to not blame the rollout for platform-wide issues.

Allison Grant: From standards perspective: please don’t make defaults too aggressive. We need to align with SLO error budget math, not arbitrary “latency > X”. Burn-rate based evaluation is best: look at error budget consumption vs SLO target, not raw metric.

Jada Williams: Controller side: we can implement “two-window confirmation” + holdoff. Example: trigger only if fast burn AND slow burn are both breaching, with min requests. Also after action (pause/rollback), don’t evaluate for N minutes to avoid bouncing.

Jordan Lee: Policy engine can express: 
- signal: latency_p99, latency_p95, error_rate_5xx
- windows: fast=5m, slow=30m
- burn_rate thresholds per window
- min_requests per window
- debounce/holdoff
Question is what default burn rate numbers should be.

Paula Mendes: Product constraint: defaults must be explainable in Console. “If it gets worse quickly AND stays worse for 30m, we stop the rollout.” That level of explanation is ok. But we can’t ship something that pages/rolls back constantly on normal warmup.

Sean Gallagher: Agree. For SRE defaults, I’m leaning:
- Latency (p99): fast 5m / slow 30m
- Error rate: fast 5m / slow 30m
Require BOTH windows to breach before rollback. But what burn rates? And do we separate tiers?

Aisha Bello: In SLO toolkit we’ve used common multiwindow burn-rate alerts:
- “page” pattern: 14.4x over 5m AND 6x over 30m (for a 99.9% SLO)
- “ticket” pattern: 3x over 2h AND 1x over 1d
We probably want something like the paging pattern for auto-rollback, but tuned down to avoid flapping + with min volume gating.

Allison Grant: +1 to those patterns. Important nuance: burn rate depends on the SLO target. If a route is 99.0 vs 99.9, the numeric burn rates mean different absolute error/latency budgets. The controller should query the monitor for burn rate status rather than re-implement math.

Rafael Mendes: Also recommend: for latency SLOs we should treat “sustained tail regression” differently than “brief warmup.” Two-window helps, but 30m slow window might still be too sensitive during ramp when baseline is shifting.

Rafael Mendes: Could we require the rollout step to be stable for, say, 10m before evaluating? Or at least avoid evaluating for the first N minutes after traffic shift.

Jada Williams: Yep, we can do “step_transition_grace_period” (aka holdoff) after any traffic change. Eg: 7m grace for canary step changes, 15m for A/B split changes.

Jordan Lee: That’s implementable. Policy fields could be:
- `evaluation_delay_after_step_change_seconds`
- `action_cooldown_seconds` (post action, suppress further actions)
We need defaults.

Sean Gallagher: Suggest defaults:
- evaluation delay: 10m after step change
- action cooldown: 30m after pause/rollback
Also: min requests. I’d like min 200 req in fast window and 2000 req in slow window (or something proportional).

Aisha Bello: For min requests, be careful across tiers. Some private deployments have low QPS, and we’ll never trigger. Maybe do:
- min_requests_fast: 100 (self-serve), 200 (dedicated), 50 (private)
- min_requests_slow: 1000 (self-serve), 2000 (dedicated), 200 (private)
But also a “fail-safe”: if volume too low, default to PAUSE not ROLLBACK, and notify.

Allison Grant: +1 on “pause vs rollback.” In the standards doc we can say: if confidence is low (low sample, monitor stale, missing baseline), pause rollout and alert owner rather than auto-rollback.

Paula Mendes: That matches MVP semantics: start with auto-pause as primary action, auto-rollback only when confidence is high.

Rafael Mendes: Another guardrail: don’t auto-rollback during known platform incident. If incidents channel has active Sev2+ tagged “telemetry” or “control-plane” we should suppress. (At least allow manual suppression.)

Jada Williams: We’re adding a suppression override anyway. Automated “do not rollback during incident” is tricky, but we can support a global suppression flag (set by oncall) that the controller respects.

Logan Wright: Jumping in from telemetry side. Metric lag is real (esp. p99) when shards are hot. If the burn rate endpoint says data is stale (>2m behind), controller should treat as “insufficient data” and not take irreversible action.

Sean Gallagher: Ok so consensus shaping:
1) Use burn-rate status from monitors (not raw thresholds)
2) Dual-window: fast=5m, slow=30m
3) Require BOTH windows breaching AND min sample sizes
4) Add holdoff after step change (10m default)
5) Add cooldown after action (30m)
6) If low confidence: pause + notify, don’t rollback
Now need actual burn-rate numbers. Proposing for eligible signals:
- Auto-rollback: 14.4x (5m) AND 6x (30m)
- Auto-pause: 6x (5m) AND 3x (30m)
Thoughts?

Aisha Bello: That’s roughly “page vs ticket” but compressed. Works. For 99.9 SLO, 14.4x is severe and should only happen with real regression. For 99.0 SLO, still meaningful because burn-rate calc accounts for target.

Allison Grant: As long as we’re consistently using monitor-provided burn rates, I’m ok. Please document that these are default multipliers and services can override within safe bounds.

Rafael Mendes: +1. Also recommend we start with auto-rollback only for error-rate SLO (5xx) in MVP, and do latency as auto-pause first. Latency is noisier and more dependent on load shape/batching.

Paula Mendes: PM view: MVP should include rollback on both latency + errors, but behind a template. Maybe default template = “Pause on latency, Rollback on errors.” Advanced template can roll back on latency.

Sean Gallagher: I like that. Default template per tier:
- Self-serve hosted: pause on latency, rollback on 5xx
- Dedicated: rollback on 5xx, pause on latency unless opted in
- Private: pause only by default (owners can enable rollback)

Jordan Lee: Config schema can support per-signal action. Something like:
- latency_p99 => action=pause
- error_rate_5xx => action=rollback
and let policy override.

Logan Wright: From observability: make sure labels don’t blow up cardinality. Don’t include raw rollout step ids on burn-rate queries in metrics. Use rollout_id only in audit events, and bounded labels for metrics.

Allison Grant: +1. Metrics for evaluation outcomes should be low-cardinality: service, route, model_family maybe, region, tier, decision=PAUSE/ROLLBACK/SUPPRESS, reason.

Rafael Mendes: Final thought: we should add “require breach for 2 consecutive evaluations” (e.g., two polls) in addition to windows. That prevents a single bad scrape from pausing.

Jada Williams: We already planned a debounce: N consecutive breaches or breach duration >= X. We can do “2 consecutive polls” given polling every 60s.

Sean Gallagher: Great. I’ll summarize for PRD/ADR:
- Default evaluation: poll every 60s
- Require 2 consecutive polls with dual-window breach
- Holdoff 10m after step change
- Cooldown 30m after action
- Default template: pause on latency, rollback on error rate
- Burn-rate thresholds: pause=6x/3x (5m/30m), rollback=14.4x/6x (5m/30m)
- Min sample sizes with tier defaults + pause-only when volume low
I’ll drop this in the doc and tag folks for review.

Aisha Bello: :thumbsup: I’ll align alert rules to match these defaults so notifications correlate.

Allison Grant: Please add a note: services can’t set burn rate thresholds lower than pause defaults (safe bounds), to prevent “rollback on tiny blips.”

Jordan Lee: Noted. We can enforce bounds in policy validation.

Rafael Mendes: :+1: and we should plan a game day scenario specifically for warmup/step-change to verify the holdoff works.

Sean Gallagher: added to game day list. Thanks all.

title: #eng-oncall: 1768865409
preview:
Rafael Mendes: Dropping the runbook draft here for review before we flip paging on phase 1. Mainly want eyes on the decision tree + escalation expectations. Doc: https://confluence.redwood.dev/eng-sre/runbooks/runbook-perf-regression-canary-alert

Rafael Mendes: TL;DR structure is:
1) Validate alert (real vs noise)
2) Classify: queueing vs runtime vs infra
3) Mitigate: rollback / disable canary / freeze
4) Escalate w/ a clean payload

Sean Gallagher: Thanks. Skimmed. It’s close, but I want it to be more “do X then Y” and less narrative. Oncall at 3am won’t read paragraphs.

Aisha Bello: +1. The decision tree graphic is helpful but we should add explicit thresholds in each branch (even if they’re “see alert payload”). E.g. “prefill_p95 up but decode flat” etc.

Noah Patel: Looking now. One thing: runbook should say what to check in the canary payload first: model, region, tier, hardware_sku, runtime_build, kernel_id, scheduler_version.

Allison Grant: Also make sure links are consistent. In the alert we’ll have Grafana panel + trace explorer + baseline artifact link. Runbook should say “start from alert links, don’t go hunting.”

Rafael Mendes: Got it. I can rewrite top section as a checklist + move the narrative to “Background” at bottom.

Sofia Ivanova: In the classifier section, we should be explicit about queueing-time interpretation. We’ve seen folks confuse “end-to-end p95” with “runtime-only p95”.

Sofia Ivanova: Suggest a table like:
- If queue_ms_p95 ↑ and runtime_ms_p95 flat → capacity/scheduling/backpressure
- If runtime_ms_p95 ↑ and queue_ms flat → kernel/runtime regression
- If both ↑ → could be fleet saturation or routing shift

Omar El-Sayed: + add “batch_size distribution changed?” That’s a smoking gun for scheduler heuristics change or sudden traffic mix shift.

Hiro Tanaka: Kernel escalation: please include when to page kernels vs file ticket. I don’t want kernel oncall spam for warn-only alerts.

Sean Gallagher: Agree. My proposal:
- Warn alert: post in #eng-runtime w/ context, no page
- Page alert: page SRE primary + runtime oncall; kernel oncall only if runtime_ms regression and kernel_id changed in same window

Aisha Bello: Can we put this in an explicit escalation matrix block? Like rows=severity, cols=teams.

Rafael Mendes: Yes. I’ll add “Escalation Matrix” section. Can someone confirm the runtime oncall alias? Is it @runtime-oncall or @serving-runtime-oncall?

Noah Patel: It’s @serving-runtime-oncall (routes to Noah/Sofia/Hiro rotation).

Allison Grant: Make sure we don’t assume access to internal dashboards. Oncall doc should mention “if links 403, ask #eng-platform-oncall or open INT ticket.” We’ve had that bite folks.

Vanessa Ortiz: Infra/fleet angle: runbook should include “check recent GPU fleet changes in region” + “check routing policy change / weight shift”. We can link to Fleet Change Log.

Vanessa Ortiz: We have a canonical dashboard for that: https://grafana.redwood.dev/d/fleet-changes (needs VPN). Add it as optional step.

Connor O'Brien: For mitigation: the runbook should state what actions are safe without release eng approval.
- Safe: pause rollout gate / hold further regions
- Needs approval: rollback runtime build, change kernel flag, change routing weights

Sean Gallagher: + include the “disable switch” for canary itself. If canary is causing load or alert storm, oncall should know how to set canary.enabled=false per region.

Rafael Mendes: Do we want oncall to disable canary in prod directly? Or only SRE can?

Sean Gallagher: SRE oncall can, but the runbook should say “only if overhead > budget OR alert storm confirmed.” Otherwise we lose signal.

Aisha Bello: Can you add a quick check for overhead? Something like “perf-canary GPU budget panel should be <0.5% per region” with the exact Grafana panel link.

Noah Patel: Another thing: the “Validate alert” step should include minimum sample size. E.g., if observed_n < min_samples, it’s likely noise. People tend to overreact.

Allison Grant: Yes, and include “anti-flap is already applied” so oncall doesn’t manually wait 30m unless the rule indicates it.

Hiro Tanaka: Small but important: when escalating to kernels, include kernel_id + arch + seq_len bucket if we have it. Otherwise we can’t reproduce.

Omar El-Sayed: We have seq_len_bucket and prompt_shape_id tags in canary metrics. Runbook should explicitly say to paste those into the thread.

Sofia Ivanova: On the decision tree, I’d also add a branch for telemetry pipeline delay. We had one case where baseline fetch lagged and the alert fired “late” with confusing timestamps.

Rafael Mendes: Good call. I’ll add: “If dashboards show flat but alert claims regression, check telemetry ingestion delay panel / statuspage.”

Connor O'Brien: Rollback guidance: runbook should mention when to revert runtime build vs when to revert config (scheduler knobs). We can’t always roll back runtime quickly.

Noah Patel: Maybe:
- If regression aligns with runtime_build change → rollback runtime (if within canary rollout window)
- If aligns with config rollout (batching heuristic) → flip feature flag / config revert
- If aligns with fleet change → engage infra, consider draining bad nodes

Sean Gallagher: + include comms expectation: for a page-level sustained regression on top models, open incident in #incidents and update every 30m. Warn-level stays in #eng-runtime thread.

Aisha Bello: Please add explicit “Definition of sustained” (e.g. 2 consecutive windows / 20 min). That’s currently implicit.

Rafael Mendes: Ack. I’ll incorporate these edits today and repost for final sign-off.

Allison Grant: When you repost, can you also add a “Copy/paste template” for the incident thread? Like:
Model:
Region:
Tier:
Hardware:
Observed:
Baseline:
Runtime build:
Kernel id:
Queue vs runtime breakdown:
Mitigation taken:

Rafael Mendes: Yep, will do.

Hiro Tanaka: Once updated, tag me on the kernel escalation section. Want to make sure it’s crisp.

Sean Gallagher: Same for SRE actions. I want to ensure we’re not asking oncall to do anything risky without guardrails.

Rafael Mendes: Sounds good. Targeting EOD. :thumbsup: (will post updated link in this thread)

Aisha Bello: :thumbsup: 

Noah Patel: :thumbsup:

Sean Gallagher: SRE oncall can, but the runbook should say “only if overhead > budget OR alert storm confirmed.” Otherwise we lose signal.

Aisha Bello: Can you add a quick check for overhead? Something like “perf-canary GPU budget panel should be <0.5% per region” with the exact Grafana panel link.

Noah Patel: Another thing: the “Validate alert” step should include minimum sample size. E.g., if observed_n < min_samples, it’s likely noise. People tend to overreact.

Allison Grant: Yes, and include “anti-flap is already applied” so oncall doesn’t manually wait 30m unless the rule indicates it.

Hiro Tanaka: Small but important: when escalating to kernels, include kernel_id + arch + seq_len bucket if we have it. Otherwise we can’t reproduce.

Omar El-Sayed: We have seq_len_bucket and prompt_shape_id tags in canary metrics. Runbook should explicitly say to paste those into the thread.

Sofia Ivanova: On the decision tree, I’d also add a branch for telemetry pipeline delay. We had one case where baseline fetch lagged and the alert fired “late” with confusing timestamps.

Rafael Mendes: Good call. I’ll add: “If dashboards show flat but alert claims regression, check telemetry ingestion delay panel / statuspage.”

Connor O'Brien: Rollback guidance: runbook should mention when to revert runtime build vs when to revert config (scheduler knobs). We can’t always roll back runtime quickly.

Noah Patel: Maybe:
- If regression aligns with runtime_build change → rollback runtime (if within canary rollout window)
- If aligns with config rollout (batching heuristic) → flip feature flag / config revert
- If aligns with fleet change → engage infra, consider draining bad nodes

Sean Gallagher: + include comms expectation: for a page-level sustained regression on top models, open incident in #incidents and update every 30m. Warn-level stays in #eng-runtime thread.

Aisha Bello: Please add explicit “Definition of sustained” (e.g. 2 consecutive windows / 20 min). That’s currently implicit.

Model:
Region:
Tier:
Hardware:
Observed:
Baseline:
Runtime build:
Kernel id:
Queue vs runtime breakdown:
Mitigation taken:

title: #architecture: 1768471900
preview:
Confluence Bot: ADR updated: ADR-042 Failover Trigger Signal Hierarchy https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ARCH/ADR-042

Miles Thornton: dropping this here for architecture review. main questions: what signals are authoritative enough to flip regions, and what guardrails we need to avoid oscillation. would love comments today/tomorrow so we can unblock ENG-2412.

Ravi Menon: skimmed. overall +1 on “reachability > 5xx burn > tail latency burn > capacity”. one nit: reachability needs to be *from where*? if it’s only from our control plane vantage points, we risk failing over for a bad ISP / partial internet issue.

Victor Alvarez: yeah, strongly agree. reachability is tricky. i’d recommend defining “authoritative reachability” as: (a) cloud internal (region-to-region) + (b) external synthetic probes from at least 2 providers/PoPs. if only one fails, it’s not authoritative.

Benji Okafor: +1. also, reachability should have a hold-down like “must be failing for N consecutive windows” AND ideally corroborate with data-plane symptoms (e.g., gateway 5xx burn or TLS handshake failures). otherwise we’ll get flip-flop when probes are flaky.

Allison Grant: on the measurement side: please be explicit which 5xx signal we treat as authoritative. app 5xx vs gateway 5xx vs upstream (model backend) errors. i’ve seen incidents where backend error spikes but gateway was fine due to retries masking it.

Jordan Lee: deterministic evaluation question: ADR says “take highest-priority signal that exceeds threshold.” what happens when reachability is “unknown” because telemetry stream is missing? defaulting unknown => unhealthy would be a disaster.

Miles Thornton: good call. my intent is unknown => “insufficient evidence”, not unhealthy. in that case the policy should fall back to next signal *only if* it’s corroborated by at least one independent signal.

Tomáš Novák: +1 on the “unknown handling” being spelled out. we’ve been paged by missing metrics before. would prefer “unknown never triggers failover” unless operator forces.

Sean Gallagher: i want to push on one thing: do we really want reachability to be highest? historically our biggest customer impact is sustained elevated 5xx + timeouts, not just probe failures. reachability probes can be noisy.

Victor Alvarez: if we don’t treat reachability as top-tier, we’ll fail over late in cases of real network partition where app metrics lag (because traffic can’t reach the region to generate 5xx). but we can tighten the definition so it’s less noisy.

Ravi Menon: maybe we separate “hard down” vs “degraded” modes? like: reachability-hard-fail => immediate flip (fast path), but reachability-soft / partial => needs corroboration with 5xx burn.

Mina Haddad: +1, also helps with partial outages. we’ve had cases where only one AZ had issues. if reachability is “region down” but it’s actually one LB path, we might move too much traffic and cause overload elsewhere.

Allison Grant: could encode this as levels: HEALTHY / DEGRADED / UNHEALTHY. reachability can drive UNHEALTHY only when (1) probe failure across >=2 PoPs AND (2) internal east-west also failing OR error burn is already elevated.

Jordan Lee: from policy-engine POV, would love it if ADR defines:
- signal “authoritativeness” (hard vs soft)
- required corroboration count
- minimum dwell time before a flip
- cooldown after flip
So we can implement deterministically and unit test.

Benji Okafor: on oscillation: the ADR currently has “dampening timers” but doesn’t say if they’re per-signal or per-action. i’d recommend:
1) per-signal debounce (e.g., 3/5 windows)
2) action hold-down (don’t flip back for X minutes unless catastrophic)
3) rate limit flips per tenant/tier (max 1 per 30m?)

Sergio Costa: +1. and log a reason code that’s stable (small enum) for dashboards. if we include raw threshold values in reason, cardinality will explode.

Miles Thornton: noted. reason codes will be like REACHABILITY_HARD_FAIL, ERROR_BURN_FAST, LATENCY_BURN_FAST, CAPACITY_EXHAUSTION, OPERATOR_OVERRIDE.

Sean Gallagher: can we make “capacity exhaustion” not an automatic failover trigger by default? we’ve seen headroom metrics lie during scale events. if we fail over on capacity signal alone, we could cascade.

Mina Haddad: agree. capacity should probably gate *how much* traffic we shift, not *whether* we shift. e.g. “if target region headroom < X, then partial failover + shed low-priority tiers.”

Benji Okafor: yes. i want explicit mention: “capacity is a safety gate and shaping input, not a primary trigger.” failover triggered by reachability/errors/latency; capacity decides blast radius.

Victor Alvarez: also: reachability failures can happen due to DNS / TTL / resolver cache weirdness. do we have a plan to distinguish “DNS wrong” vs “region down”? maybe include separate DNS health (authoritative resolvers + anycast).

Allison Grant: we can add a “network/DNS reachability” panel, but i’m wary of adding another top-tier signal without clarity. maybe fold into reachability bucket but require internal + external agreement.

Jordan Lee: for “prevent oscillation”: is rollback handled in ADR-043? if so, ADR-042 should at least reference the interface: i.e., this ADR decides *when to flip*, ADR-043 decides *when to revert*.

Miles Thornton: yep, ADR-043 is coming next. i’ll add a section in ADR-042: “this ADR does not define rollback; see ADR-043.” and will clarify unknown-handling + hard/soft reachability.

Sean Gallagher: request: include explicit operator mode guidance. e.g. “automation in prod requires corroboration >=2 signals” vs “operator override can flip with 1 signal.” we don’t want oncall to assume the system will do something it won’t.

Tomáš Novák: +1 and please document “do not fail over” scenarios. ex: single model backend regression in one region shouldn’t flip all Hosted API routes.

Ravi Menon: last point: tail latency burn can be a side effect of retry storms. if we trigger failover on latency burn, we might amplify. can we require latency burn + elevated 5xx OR latency burn + reachability degraded?

Miles Thornton: this is super helpful. action items i’m taking for ADR text:
- define reachability hard vs soft and required corroboration
- unknown signal semantics (unknown != unhealthy)
- capacity as safety gate/shaping, not standalone trigger
- oscillation: per-signal debounce + action hold-down + rate limits (with refs to ADR-043)
- add explicit operator vs automation corroboration rules
will push an updated draft EOD and re-tag.

Confluence Bot: Miles Thornton edited ADR-042 Failover Trigger Signal Hierarchy https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ARCH/ADR-042

Allison Grant: once it’s updated, ping me and i’ll sanity check the metrics definitions so we don’t bake in ambiguous “5xx” language.

Victor Alvarez: i’ll add a comment in-page with a concrete reachability criteria proposal (PoPs/providers/internal).
