ci-bot: Step timings:
- bundle-verify-integration: 6m12s (was 33m41s)

Sahana Iyer: ✅ perf is back. i’ll file a follow-up to add a nightly “full-size bundle smoke” so we still catch weird tar/unpack issues without slowing main.

Connor O'Brien: perfect. this unblocks the release train gate. thanks.

title: #docs: 1759920184
preview:
Elliot Price: quick q for the status page transparency work — docs is updating the public “how to read incidents” section + post-incident summary format. What can we say publicly when the incident is Dedicated or Private? We keep getting stuck on wording.

Wei Chen: +1. Specifically: do we ever say “Dedicated customers impacted” on the public status page, or do we always keep it to Hosted API components only?

Kira Thompson: we should split Dedicated vs Private. Dedicated is tricky bc there’s shared control plane signals + customer-isolated data plane. Private (VPC/on-prem) is customer-managed env; generally shouldn’t show up on public status page unless it’s our control plane / update service / license service affecting multiple customers.

Elliot Price: ok. so: Dedicated data plane incident for a single customer: no public status update?

Dev Patel: from Support POV: please no. customers hate seeing “everything is fine” on the public page when they’re down, but also we can’t publicly confirm “Customer X dedicated cluster is having capacity issues.” For single-customer Dedicated issues, we handle via ticket + direct comms.

Wei Chen: can we at least say “Some Dedicated customers may be impacted” when we’re not sure if it’s one vs many?

Sean Gallagher: careful. guidance we’ve been pushing in SRE is:
- Public status page is for shared surfaces + broadly scoped impact.
- If Dedicated incident is limited to one tenant/cluster, treat as customer-specific and do not post publicly.
- If impact crosses multiple Dedicated tenants due to shared control plane, shared image rollout, shared auth, shared routing, etc — then yes, we can post a Dedicated component incident.

Kira Thompson: + the copy should avoid implying SLA commitment. Don’t say “within 15 minutes we’ll update”, say “we’ll share updates as we learn more” etc.

Wei Chen: makes sense. what about Dedicated but only in one region? (e.g. “Dedicated - us-east”)

Marissa Cole: product/design direction is: public components can include region where it’s meaningful and non-sensitive. For Dedicated we’ll likely keep it “Dedicated Control Plane” and (maybe) “Dedicated Provisioning” — not per-customer clusters. If there’s a region-wide shared service issue (e.g. provisioning API in us-east), we can say region.

Elliot Price: can we name the region for Private?

Naomi Feldman: only if it’s our shared service / Redwood-managed plane and the region is a Redwood region (e.g. “us-east”). Do NOT disclose customer VPC details, cloud account details, or “air-gapped on-prem customer” etc. Also avoid implying we can see inside customer networks.

Michael Grant: legal note: avoid any statement that could be interpreted as confirming a particular customer’s outage (“Customer environments are down”). Stick to “our control plane service is degraded” / “some customers may experience issues” phrasing. Also avoid “data loss” unless confirmed and approved.

Wei Chen: ok, so what can we safely say for Private if it’s NOT shared? Like customer’s on-prem deployment has an issue.

Kira Thompson: generally: don’t post it publicly. Docs can say: “Private deployments may not be reflected on the public status page. For Private incidents, we will communicate directly through your agreed support channels.”

Dev Patel: +1 please include that sentence. It sets expectation and helps deflect tickets.

Elliot Price: for Dedicated: similar line? “Dedicated incidents may be reported on the status page when they involve shared Redwood-managed components”?

Sean Gallagher: yup. we also need to keep it consistent with the new incident comms templates. We should avoid “we are experiencing X” if it’s limited scope; say “Some customers may experience…” until scope is confirmed.

Wei Chen: can we draft some approved boilerplate for each?

Elliot Price: proposing doc snippets (tell me if ok):
1) Dedicated:
- “Dedicated capacity incidents are reflected on the status page when they affect shared Redwood-managed services (e.g., control plane, provisioning, authentication) or multiple Dedicated tenants. Issues isolated to a single Dedicated deployment are communicated directly to the affected customer(s).”
2) Private:
- “Private (VPC/on-prem) deployments may not be represented on the public status page. When a Private incident is isolated to a specific customer environment, Redwood will communicate directly through your support contact and agreed channels. If a shared Redwood-managed service impacts multiple Private deployments, we may post an update on the status page.”

Naomi Feldman: security-approved in principle. Just avoid listing examples that suggest visibility into customer infra. Your examples are fine since they’re our services.

Michael Grant: legal tweak: “may not be represented” is good. Also recommend “we may post an update” instead of “we will”. No promises.

Kira Thompson: also add: “We won’t share customer-identifying details.” Simple and explicit.

Wei Chen: + for status updates themselves, suggested language blocks?

Sean Gallagher: for Dedicated/Private shared-service incidents:
- Investigating: “We’re investigating reports of elevated errors affecting [Dedicated Control Plane] for some customers.”
- Identified: “We’ve identified an issue with [service class] and are working on mitigation.”
- Mitigating: “A mitigation has been applied and we’re monitoring recovery.”
Avoid: “capacity shortfall”, “tenant”, “noisy neighbor”, specific customer counts.

Dev Patel: and avoid “all Dedicated customers impacted” unless we have hard evidence.

Elliot Price: great. I’ll incorporate this into docs + link to the comms template page. Will also add a callout box: “If you’re seeing an issue not reflected on the status page…” with guidance to contact support.

Wei Chen: can we get a final thumbs up from Kira + Sean on that paragraph before we merge the docs PR?

Kira Thompson: yes, send me the diff.

Sean Gallagher: +1, drop it in #eng-sre too so Comms Leads see same wording.

Elliot Price: will do. thanks all. :thumbsup:

Michael Grant: (last note) if you add post-incident summary guidance, include a line like “We may not include customer-specific details” and avoid listing exact affected customer names/log IDs.

Elliot Price: ack. adding.

title: #eng-sre: 1759882210
preview:
Rafael Mendes: Kicking off a thread on status page component taxonomy. We need to stop calling everything “API” :grimacing:. Proposal: split Hosted API vs Streaming vs Embeddings (and maybe Reranking) + split by region. Want feedback on shape + naming.

Christine Wong: +1. Current single “API” component is killing us in comms. Customers see “Operational” while embeddings is on fire.

Mina Haddad: For region split, do we want cloud region names (us-east-1/us-west-2/eu-west-1) or friendly (US East/US West/EU West)? Friendly is better for customers, but mapping has to be unambiguous.

Allison Grant: Observability side: we can tag metrics by `region` already; the gateway has `pop` and `cluster` labels. Friendly display name is fine as long as component_id uses canonical region key.

Aisha Bello: If we do product x region, number of components blows up quickly. What’s our target component count? Statuspage UI gets unwieldy >~25 components.

Rafael Mendes: Right now we’re thinking: only split regions for customer-facing request paths (Hosted API + Streaming + Embeddings + Reranking). Console stays global. Dedicated/Private handled separately/limited exposure.

Miles Thornton: +1 to keeping it mostly a 2D grid. Also recommend we treat Streaming as capability, not just “Chat”. It’s a distinct failure mode (disconnects, stalled tokens) even when non-streaming completions look fine.

Christine Wong: Draft list (WIP):
- Hosted API (US East)
- Hosted API (US West)
- Hosted API (EU West)
- Streaming (US East)
- Streaming (US West)
- Streaming (EU West)
- Embeddings (US East)
- Embeddings (US West)
- Embeddings (EU West)
- Reranking (US East)
- Reranking (US West)
- Reranking (EU West)
- Console (Global)

Mina Haddad: That’s 13 components. That feels manageable.

Aisha Bello: For Hosted API, are we mixing chat + text generation? If a model router issue breaks only chat routes, we’ll still over-report.

Rafael Mendes: Good q. My bias: keep Hosted API as “non-streaming text generation/chat request acceptance” and call out route-level details in incident updates. We can add a “Chat” component later if needed, but I’d rather not go to 20+ right away.

Allison Grant: We *can* separate chat vs completions based on route tags (`/v1/chat/completions` vs `/v1/completions`). But we’d need consistency across SDKs + gateway normalization.

Logan Wright: Gateway emits `route_name` today but it’s messy (some are templated, some are raw paths). If we make “Chat” a public component, we need clean cardinality + a mapping table.

Irene Choi: Infra perspective: splitting chat vs text gen might create more confusion unless we have super crisp definitions. Customers think “chat” = their product, not our endpoint.

Dev Patel: Support POV: the #1 complaint is “embeddings failing but status page green.” #2 is “streaming disconnects but you say API operational.” I haven’t seen many tickets asking for chat vs completions split.

Sean Gallagher: Agree w/ Dev. Let’s fix the high-signal pain first: embeddings + streaming as first-class components, split by region. We can iterate once we have accuracy audits.

Aisha Bello: For region grouping, can we align to the 3 regions customers commonly use: US East, US West, EU? What about APAC?

Mina Haddad: APAC usage exists but it’s mostly via partner/edge right now; traffic often routes to US West anyway. We can add APAC once we have a distinct region/control plane.

Allison Grant: From dashboards side, we currently have clean partitions for `us-east`, `us-west`, `eu-west`. APAC would be “other” today. I’d rather not publish “Other”.

Christine Wong: Naming: “Hosted API” vs “API Requests” vs “Inference API”? Trying to avoid ambiguity w/ Dedicated.

Rafael Mendes: Proposal:
- “Hosted API” for standard request/response
- “Hosted Streaming” for SSE streaming
- “Hosted Embeddings”
- “Hosted Reranking”
Then in incident templates we’ll say “Hosted API (US East) experiencing elevated 5xx”.

Dev Patel: Please keep “Hosted” in the name. Otherwise enterprise customers think Dedicated is included in the same component.

Miles Thornton: +1, and component_ids should include deployment mode:
`hosted_api_us_east`, `hosted_streaming_us_east`, `hosted_embeddings_us_east`, etc.
Display names: “Hosted API (US East)”.

Logan Wright: Mapping rule suggestion (so we don’t fight this during incidents):
- If request has `capability=embeddings` => embeddings component
- Else if `streaming=true` => streaming component
- Else => hosted_api
Edge case: rerank is non-streaming but not “api” (customers care).

Allison Grant: Also need clear region selection rule:
- Use serving cluster region (where inference happened), not where customer originated.
Otherwise status can show US East impacted even if we failed over to US West.

Mina Haddad: Agree. Region should represent where we’re serving from / where capacity is impacted.

Aisha Bello: How do we handle partial failures (e.g., only some models)? We won’t have per-model components.

Sean Gallagher: That’s OK. Component degraded + incident text should specify “some models” / “subset of traffic” without listing customer names or capacity details.

Christine Wong: I’ll add definition blurbs:
- Hosted API: non-streaming generation + request acceptance
- Hosted Streaming: streaming responses (SSE) delivery and token streaming
- Hosted Embeddings: embedding generation
- Hosted Reranking: rerank endpoint

Dev Patel: Also add “what it does NOT include” for each (e.g., Console, Dedicated). Helps support macros.

Rafael Mendes: Action items:
1) Christine to turn this into canonical taxonomy doc + component definitions.
2) Mina/Logan to propose region + mapping rules (cluster region).
3) Allison/Aisha to sanity check dashboards/alerts exist per component.
Goal: ready for PR on component registry this week.

Allison Grant: I’ll confirm the metric queries. We should include both error rate + latency p95 for “degraded” criteria, and hysteresis to avoid flapping.

Miles Thornton: Also think about grouping on Statuspage UI: group by product (Hosted API/Streaming/Embeddings/Reranking) then by region underneath.

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: Let’s proceed with the 13-component list. If someone feels strongly about adding “Chat” as a separate component, propose it with ticket + evidence.

Christine Wong: Cool, I’ll draft the doc and post here for review EOD. (Will include the component_id naming + ownership placeholders.)

title: #eng-oncall: 1749564412
preview:
Sean Gallagher: seeing a short spike in 429s starting ~2 mins ago. looks tied to benchmark launch traffic. anyone else seeing this?

Incident Bot: *Alert* `bench-results-service` elevated 429 rate
- window: last 5m
- 429 ratio: 3.8% (baseline <0.2%)
- top path: `/v1/benchmarks/results?format=json`
- region: us-east (edge)
- dashboard: https://grafana.redwood.ai/d/bench-results

Neha Kapoor: yup, I’m on the same dashboard. p95 looks ok-ish but 429s are real. is this our edge rate limiter or app-level?

Sean Gallagher: looks like edge token bucket. rate limit header present, `x-redwood-ratelimit-policy=public-results-anon`.

Felix Schneider: fwiw the interactive table hits results endpoint a lot on first load + when people flip filters quickly. we also have some prefetch for “top models” that might be too aggressive.

Logan Wright: analytics shows a ton of repeated loads from same IP blocks (likely scrapers + people refreshing). top ASNs are a couple cloud providers.

Omar El-Sayed: quick check in logs: app is returning 200s fine. 429s are before it hits service (no request IDs in app logs). so this is definitely edge/WAF policy.

Vanessa Ortiz: any chance we’re saturating capacity and edge is “protecting” downstream? or is it a hard limit unrelated to fleet?

Sean Gallagher: hard limit. currently 60 req/min per IP for anon on that path. during the spike we’re seeing ~200-400 req/min from a handful of IPs.

Neha Kapoor: we should be careful about loosening globally and getting scraped to death. can we split policy: higher limit for CDN-cached artifact path + keep strict for uncached query endpoint?

Felix Schneider: currently the page requests `/v1/benchmarks/results?format=json` which is dynamic (though effectively versioned by `release=2025-07-10`). we could switch to fetching the versioned artifact URL and let CDN handle it.

Alicia Perez: from web side: we can ship a quick patch to use the versioned JSON asset if that helps. but that’s not immediate.

Harish Reddy: if we raise limit for an hour, any risk to other prod traffic? launch is hot right now.

Sean Gallagher: risk is mostly increased load on the results service + cache layer. but right now 429 is blocking legit users too. we can do a scoped bump (public-results-anon only) and tighten back after peak.

Neha Kapoor: proposal: bump anon limit from 60 -> 180 req/min per IP for 2 hours, AND add a second rule blocking obvious scraper behavior (e.g., >X distinct query params per minute). do we have that capability fast?

Sean Gallagher: fast path is just bumping the token bucket. smarter rule needs config review (and might false positive). I’d rather bump now + monitor origin RPS + error rate.

Omar El-Sayed: +1. origin can handle more. current origin RPS is ~35, would maybe go to 80-100. still fine.

Logan Wright: also: a lot of the requests are identical. cache hit ratio at edge for that endpoint is low because of query params ordering (some callers send `format=json&release=...` vs `release=...&format=json`). might be hurting us.

Felix Schneider: oof yeah. we can normalize query param order on client, but not today. if edge caching key is strict, we’re missing cache.

Sean Gallagher: ok I’m going to bump `public-results-anon` to 180 rpm and add a note in #incidents thread. if origin starts to sweat, we revert.

Incident Bot: *Change applied* `edge-rate-limit/public-results-anon`
- previous: 60 req/min/IP
- new: 180 req/min/IP
- requested by: Sean Gallagher

Neha Kapoor: watching graphs. 429s dropping already.

Logan Wright: 429 ratio now 0.6% and falling. CTA clicks are up again, so user impact likely resolved.

Vanessa Ortiz: any need for extra fleet capacity as a result of this? (I can poke Harish if we need reserves.)

Sean Gallagher: not yet. keep an eye on origin CPU + cache. if we cross 70% sustained we’ll consider scaling.

Felix Schneider: action item for tomorrow: move interactive page to fetch versioned artifact (static URL) + reduce prefetch. I’ll open a ticket and tag you all.

Neha Kapoor: also add `Retry-After` header on 429 if we’re not already—helps clients behave.

Omar El-Sayed: edge is already adding `retry-after: 1` (saw it in curl). good there.

Sean Gallagher: cool. leaving the bumped limit in place until 2 hrs post-peak unless we see abuse. please ping if 429s creep back.

Neha Kapoor: :thumbsup:

Harish Reddy: :eyes: monitoring. ping me if we need to scale cache/origin.

Alicia Perez: thanks all — if you want us to add a banner/tooltip about “don’t refresh like crazy” we can, but hopefully unnecessary.

Sean Gallagher: lol. let’s avoid that. better to fix the fetch/caching path.

Neha Kapoor: agreed. closing loop: short-term mitigation done. long-term fix is static artifact fetch + cache key normalization + prefetch tuning.

preview:
Camila Reyes: It’s live. :mega:

Redwood’s **Latency/$ Benchmark Report** is now public.

**Landing page + interactive tables:** https://redwoodinference.com/benchmarks/latency-per-dollar
**Download PDF:** https://redwoodinference.com/benchmarks/latency-per-dollar/report.pdf
**Methodology (public):** https://redwoodinference.com/benchmarks/latency-per-dollar/methodology

Huge shoutout to Applied ML, Runtime, Infra, Web, PMM, Legal, and Sales Enablement for getting this over the line. Please use this thread for quick questions + what you’re seeing in the wild.

Key ask: if you share externally, please stick to the approved talk track + don’t ad-lib comparative claims beyond what’s in the report.

Soojin Lee: Congrats everyone. :raised_hands:

**Internal enablement kit (Sales/SE/CS):**
- Core message house + disallowed phrasing: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/SE/benchmark-report-core-message-house
- FAQ + objection handling: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/SE/benchmark-report-faq
- Competitive notes (safe language): https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/SE/benchmark-competitive-notes
- Internal launch deck (for customer calls): https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/SE/benchmark-report-launch-deck
- One-pager (send to AEs/SDRs): https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/redwood-gtm/benchmark-one-pager

**CTA guidance (quick):**
- Developers / PLG: push landing page + “try Hosted API”
- Enterprise / security / SLOs: “Dedicated sizing” or “Private/VPC demo” (don’t over-index on a single chart)

If you’re unsure how to position for a specific prospect, tag @Markus Klein or @Stephanie Nguyen / @Lukas Meyer.

Ethan Wallace: Adding press/social assets for anyone who needs them:
- Approved visuals + social copy snippets: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/redwood-gtm/benchmark-press-kit
- Blog post (public): https://redwoodinference.com/blog/latency-per-dollar-benchmarks

If you see comments about “fairness” or “cherry-picking,” please link methodology first and avoid debating in threads. We have a short response template in the FAQ.

Jonas Weber: From the technical side: please route methodology questions to the public methodology page above.

Two reminders that came up in reviews:
- We publish **p50/p95 + latency/$ + $/1M tokens**, not just a single “winner” number.
- The report is a **defined workload mix** (prefill/decode) with stated concurrency + warmups; results will vary by prompt distribution.

If a prospect wants to replicate, we have a lightweight “PoC replication notes” section in the enablement FAQ.

Markus Klein: For SEs doing live walkthroughs: use the interactive table first (filters + tooltips), then PDF for narrative.

Mini demo flow that’s been working:
1) Pick their region + model family
2) Toggle p95 latency + latency/$
3) Talk about Dedicated/Private options for isolation + predictable throughput

Also: if someone asks “is caching enabled?” don’t guess—use the exact language in methodology.

Stephanie Nguyen: For NA AEs/SDRs: the recommended first-touch email is in Outreach as **“Benchmark Launch - Latency/$”** (updated this morning). Please don’t forward the draft doc versions.

If you get replies asking “can you guarantee these numbers?”, the correct answer is: we guarantee **SLA targets** on Dedicated (latency/uptime) and we can run a PoC in their region/workload to validate.

Lukas Meyer: EMEA note: we’re getting immediate questions on data residency. Please stick to:
- “We offer EU regions; see the region selector in the interactive table.”
- “For stricter requirements, Redwood Private (VPC/on-prem) is available; we can align on residency + controls.”

Do not claim “all data stays in X” unless the deal has the right deployment + contract language.

Grace O'Connor: SDR routing: inbound demo requests from the benchmark page are tagged **Campaign = Benchmark Launch** in HubSpot. If you see one untagged, please drop the link in #revops-help.

Jasmine Liu: CS/support heads-up: expect tickets asking “why did my run differ from the report?”

Suggested response:
- Point to methodology + note workload variance
- Offer to help them run a small test with their prompt set
- Escalate anything that smells like a public claim discrepancy to #benchmark-war-room

Sean Gallagher: SRE note: we’re monitoring landing page + JSON results service closely this week.

If you see the public page loading slowly or errors, please post in #eng-sre with:
- URL
- approx time
- screenshot

(We’ve got dashboards + alerts live.)

LaunchBot: Launch week schedule (PT)
- Tue 10:00am: Community post + HN monitoring
- Wed 9:00am: Lifecycle email send (PLG)
- Thu 11:00am: Webinar (live demo + Q&A)
- Fri 2:00pm: Sales/SE office hours (internal)

Full calendar: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/redwood-gtm/benchmark-editorial-calendar

Camila Reyes: Final note: please amplify, but be precise. If you’re posting externally, use the approved visuals and link the landing page (not random screenshots).

Proud of this team.

title: #architecture: 1749014421
preview:
Victor Alvarez: Kicking off arch review thread re: VPC endpoints defaults + DNS behavior for the alpha Terraform modules. Main open question: do we enable private DNS on interface endpoints by default, and how does installer validate prerequisites?

Irene Choi: +1. We need something that “just works” for typical enterprise private subnets, but doesn’t silently break when customer has custom DNS / private hosted zones.

Nina Petrova: From the Private installer side: the top failure mode in pilots is ECR pull failing (no endpoint + no NAT) and then people assume it’s “Redwood broken”. We should bias defaults toward endpoints ON.

Marcus Lin: Security angle: prefer endpoints over NAT egress, esp for regulated. But if we do endpoints, we need to be careful about endpoint policies + SG rules so we’re not punching wide holes.

Victor Alvarez: Current proposal (module defaults):
- S3: Gateway endpoint attached to private route tables
- ECR API + ECR DKR: Interface endpoints
- STS: Interface endpoint
- CloudWatch Logs: Interface endpoint
- KMS: Interface endpoint (optional?)
- EC2 + Autoscaling + ELB: optional / depends on if they run EKS private endpoint + node lifecycle needs
- SSM: optional (nice-to-have for ops)

Enable `private_dns_enabled=true` on interface endpoints by default.

Hanae Suzuki: DNS gotchas: enabling private DNS for ECR endpoints can conflict if customer has a private hosted zone for `ecr.<region>.amazonaws.com` (seen in some locked-down shops). But I think default ON is still correct, just document override.

Simran Kaur: In reference architecture doc we say “no public egress required for image pulls” if endpoints exist. That implicitly assumes private DNS is enabled, otherwise resolving `*.dkr.ecr...` still goes public, right?

Victor Alvarez: Yep. Without private DNS, clients need to use the endpoint-specific DNS names (the long `vpce-xxxx` names). Nobody does that for ECR.

Sean Gallagher: Operationally: installer should fail fast if it can’t pull images / reach control plane deps. Don’t wait until half the cluster is up.

Rafael Mendes: Agree. We should bake preflight checks into installer (or separate “network verify” command). Like: “from inside subnet, can you resolve and connect to ECR, STS, S3?”.

Nina Petrova: Small nuance: installer itself runs wherever customer runs it (often a CI runner or a laptop via VPN). So checks that rely on being inside private subnet can be misleading.

Bruno Silva: We can do AWS API checks too though. Like describe endpoints in VPC and validate they exist + private DNS enabled. That doesn’t prove DNS resolution inside VPC, but catches 80%.

Irene Choi: Can we do both?
1) AWS API validation (DescribeVpcEndpoints + attributes)
2) If installer is running on an instance in the VPC (common in enterprise), do runtime DNS/connectivity checks.

Victor Alvarez: That’s what I was thinking.

Marcus Lin: On “private DNS enabled by default”: +1, but we should also validate VPC flags: `enableDnsSupport` and `enableDnsHostnames`. If those are off, private DNS won’t work even if endpoints exist.

Victor Alvarez: Good call. Terraform module can set those on the VPC resource, but if customer is using an existing VPC we need to either enforce or document.

Kira Thompson: Docs request: spell out “if you bring-your-own VPC, you must have enableDnsSupport/Hostnames = true” and “if you have custom DHCP options / on-prem DNS, check resolver rules.” This comes up every discovery.

Simran Kaur: Also EKS private endpoint mode: for private cluster, you still need certain AWS APIs reachable from nodes (ECR/STS/EC2). If we don’t include EC2 endpoint, node bootstrap can break depending on how hard they lock egress.

Nina Petrova: For Redwood Private, we assume EKS (at least in alpha reference arch). Nodes definitely call EC2 APIs during bootstrapping + CNI. If no NAT, you need more endpoints than just ECR.

Victor Alvarez: So maybe the “required” endpoint set for alpha should be bigger:
- ecr.api
- ecr.dkr
- s3 (gateway)
- sts
- logs
- ec2
And “recommended”:
- kms (if KMS-encrypted stuff)
- autoscaling (if managed nodegroups)
- elasticloadbalancing (if using NLB/ALB)
- monitoring (CloudWatch metrics)
- ssm + ssmmessages + ec2messages (for SSM)

Marcus Lin: Careful with “required”: if they allow NAT egress, they can skip endpoints. But for “no public egress” posture, yes, that’s the required set.

Sean Gallagher: Can we encode that as installer output?
- Detect NAT route to IGW? (well, NAT implies route to NAT in private RT)
- If no NAT present, then enforce endpoints set
- If NAT present, warn if endpoints missing but don’t fail

Bruno Silva: NAT detection via AWS APIs is possible (describe route tables for `0.0.0.0/0 -> nat-...`). Not perfect but good.

Rafael Mendes: Another check: verify endpoints are in the same subnets as the cluster nodes (or at least reachable). We’ve seen endpoints created only in one AZ/subnet and then cross-AZ traffic gets weird / costs.

Victor Alvarez: Module default will create interface endpoints in all private subnets (one per AZ). But yes, installer can verify `SubnetIds` covers expected.

Hanae Suzuki: Endpoint SG rules: we should keep it simple. The endpoint ENIs should allow 443 inbound from node SG (or from private subnet CIDRs). Least privilege is nice, but if we get it wrong we strand people.

Marcus Lin: Least privilege suggestion: allow 443 from “cluster nodes SG” + “installer/bastion SG” if provided. Avoid CIDR-wide if we can. But we need an escape hatch.

Irene Choi: For alpha, I’d rather have a working default than ultra-tight SG logic that’s fragile. But we can still do SG-to-SG.

Victor Alvarez: Proposed endpoint SG default:
- Ingress: tcp/443 from `var.allowed_client_security_group_ids` (default: node SG, and optionally a shared “private clients” SG)
- Egress: allow all (AWS endpoints are managed anyway)

Kira Thompson: How do we explain private DNS risk to customers? We should include a doc note:
- If you already manage private hosted zones for `amazonaws.com` names, set `private_dns_enabled=false` and configure Route53 resolver rules accordingly.

Simran Kaur: + link to AWS docs on endpoint private DNS + split-horizon. (People mix it up with “private hosted zone”.)

Nina Petrova: Installer-side: please add explicit error strings. Something like:
"Missing VPC endpoint for ECR (com.amazonaws.<region>.ecr.dkr). If your private subnets have no NAT egress, image pulls will fail. Fix: enable VPC endpoint + private DNS or add NAT/proxy."

Sean Gallagher: Can we include a one-liner command in the error that works from inside cluster?
`nslookup <acct>.dkr.ecr.<region>.amazonaws.com`
and
`curl -I https://api.ecr.<region>.amazonaws.com`

Bruno Silva: We can put that into troubleshooting section. Preflight can also run a basic DNS resolution check if it’s inside VPC.

Victor Alvarez: Rough installer preflight pseudo-output:
```
[network] vpc_id=vpc-1234
[network] dns_support=true dns_hostnames=true
[network] nat_detected=false
[network] checking required endpoints (no NAT detected)
  - ecr.api: OK (vpce-aaa, private_dns=true, subnets=3)
  - ecr.dkr: FAIL (not found)
  - sts: OK (vpce-bbb, private_dns=true)
  - logs: OK (vpce-ccc, private_dns=true)
  - s3 gateway: OK (vpce-ddd, rt_associations=3)
Result: FAIL. Missing endpoints: ecr.dkr
```

Marcus Lin: Looks good. Also check endpoint policy is not overly restrictive? Hard to validate generically. Maybe just warn if policy is non-default and we see explicit Deny?

Irene Choi: That might be overreach for alpha. But we can at least surface “endpoint policy present” in debug output.

Rafael Mendes: One more DNS item: if they use on-prem DNS via inbound resolver endpoints, and they forward `amazonaws.com` zones, they can break private DNS. Installer should tell them: “check Route53 resolver rules / DHCP options domain-name-servers.”

Hanae Suzuki: +1. We can’t introspect that fully, but we can add a targeted warning when private DNS is enabled and VPC has custom DHCP options set (domain-name-servers not AmazonProvidedDNS).

Victor Alvarez: Nice. That’s detectable.

Nina Petrova: Action items from this thread:
1) Module defaults: endpoints ON, private DNS ON for interface endpoints, S3 gateway for S3
2) Document override for private DNS + hosted zone conflicts
3) Installer preflight: describe endpoints + privateDnsEnabled + VPC DNS flags + NAT detection; fail if missing required endpoints when no NAT
4) Troubleshooting text for DNS resolution + on-prem resolver forwarding pitfalls

Irene Choi: Works for me. Victor can you capture in ADR comment + ping Bruno (module) + Nina (installer) so we’re aligned?

Victor Alvarez: Yep. I’ll summarize + add to the PR notes. :thumbsup:

Sean Gallagher: + please make sure preflight runs before any expensive provisioning steps. We want fast feedback.

Victor Alvarez: Noted. Closing this out unless more concerns.

Sean Gallagher: Operationally: installer should fail fast if it can’t pull images / reach control plane deps. Don’t wait until half the cluster is up.

Rafael Mendes: Agree. We should bake preflight checks into installer (or separate “network verify” command). Like: “from inside subnet, can you resolve and connect to ECR, STS, S3?”.

Nina Petrova: Small nuance: installer itself runs wherever customer runs it (often a CI runner or a laptop via VPN). So checks that rely on being inside private subnet can be misleading.

Bruno Silva: We can do AWS API checks too though. Like describe endpoints in VPC and validate they exist + private DNS enabled. That doesn’t prove DNS resolution inside VPC, but catches 80%.

Irene Choi: Can we do both?
1) AWS API validation (DescribeVpcEndpoints + attributes)
2) If installer is running on an instance in the VPC (common in enterprise), do runtime DNS/connectivity checks.

Victor Alvarez: That’s what I was thinking.

Marcus Lin: On “private DNS enabled by default”: +1, but we should also validate VPC flags: `enableDnsSupport` and `enableDnsHostnames`. If those are off, private DNS won’t work even if endpoints exist.

Victor Alvarez: Good call. Terraform module can set those on the VPC resource, but if customer is using an existing VPC we need to either enforce or document.

Kira Thompson: Docs request: spell out “if you bring-your-own VPC, you must have enableDnsSupport/Hostnames = true” and “if you have custom DHCP options / on-prem DNS, check resolver rules.” This comes up every discovery.

Simran Kaur: Also EKS private endpoint mode: for private cluster, you still need certain AWS APIs reachable from nodes (ECR/STS/EC2). If we don’t include EC2 endpoint, node bootstrap can break depending on how hard they lock egress.

Nina Petrova: For Redwood Private, we assume EKS (at least in alpha reference arch). Nodes definitely call EC2 APIs during bootstrapping + CNI. If no NAT, you need more endpoints than just ECR.

Victor Alvarez: So maybe the “required” endpoint set for alpha should be bigger:
- ecr.api
- ecr.dkr
- s3 (gateway)
- sts
- logs
- ec2
And “recommended”:
- kms (if KMS-encrypted stuff)
- autoscaling (if managed nodegroups)
- elasticloadbalancing (if using NLB/ALB)
- monitoring (CloudWatch metrics)
- ssm + ssmmessages + ec2messages (for SSM)

Marcus Lin: Careful with “required”: if they allow NAT egress, they can skip endpoints. But for “no public egress” posture, yes, that’s the required set.

Sean Gallagher: Can we encode that as installer output?
- Detect NAT route to IGW? (well, NAT implies route to NAT in private RT)
- If no NAT present, then enforce endpoints set
- If NAT present, warn if endpoints missing but don’t fail

Bruno Silva: NAT detection via AWS APIs is possible (describe route tables for `0.0.0.0/0 -> nat-...`). Not perfect but good.

Rafael Mendes: Another check: verify endpoints are in the same subnets as the cluster nodes (or at least reachable). We’ve seen endpoints created only in one AZ/subnet and then cross-AZ traffic gets weird / costs.

Victor Alvarez: Module default will create interface endpoints in all private subnets (one per AZ). But yes, installer can verify `SubnetIds` covers expected.

Hanae Suzuki: Endpoint SG rules: we should keep it simple. The endpoint ENIs should allow 443 inbound from node SG (or from private subnet CIDRs). Least privilege is nice, but if we get it wrong we strand people.

Marcus Lin: Least privilege suggestion: allow 443 from “cluster nodes SG” + “installer/bastion SG” if provided. Avoid CIDR-wide if we can. But we need an escape hatch.

Irene Choi: For alpha, I’d rather have a working default than ultra-tight SG logic that’s fragile. But we can still do SG-to-SG.

Victor Alvarez: Proposed endpoint SG default:
- Ingress: tcp/443 from `var.allowed_client_security_group_ids` (default: node SG, and optionally a shared “private clients” SG)
- Egress: allow all (AWS endpoints are managed anyway)

Kira Thompson: How do we explain private DNS risk to customers? We should include a doc note:
- If you already manage private hosted zones for `amazonaws.com` names, set `private_dns_enabled=false` and configure Route53 resolver rules accordingly.

Simran Kaur: + link to AWS docs on endpoint private DNS + split-horizon. (People mix it up with “private hosted zone”.)

Nina Petrova: Installer-side: please add explicit error strings. Something like:
"Missing VPC endpoint for ECR (com.amazonaws.<region>.ecr.dkr). If your private subnets have no NAT egress, image pulls will fail. Fix: enable VPC endpoint + private DNS or add NAT/proxy."

Sean Gallagher: Can we include a one-liner command in the error that works from inside cluster?
`nslookup <acct>.dkr.ecr.<region>.amazonaws.com`
and
`curl -I https://api.ecr.<region>.amazonaws.com`

Bruno Silva: We can put that into troubleshooting section. Preflight can also run a basic DNS resolution check if it’s inside VPC.

Victor Alvarez: Rough installer preflight pseudo-output:
```
[network] vpc_id=vpc-1234
[network] dns_support=true dns_hostnames=true
[network] nat_detected=false
[network] checking required endpoints (no NAT detected)
  - ecr.api: OK (vpce-aaa, private_dns=true, subnets=3)
  - ecr.dkr: FAIL (not found)
  - sts: OK (vpce-bbb, private_dns=true)
  - logs: OK (vpce-ccc, private_dns=true)
  - s3 gateway: OK (vpce-ddd, rt_associations=3)
Result: FAIL. Missing endpoints: ecr.dkr
```

Marcus Lin: Looks good. Also check endpoint policy is not overly restrictive? Hard to validate generically. Maybe just warn if policy is non-default and we see explicit Deny?

Irene Choi: That might be overreach for alpha. But we can at least surface “endpoint policy present” in debug output.

Rafael Mendes: One more DNS item: if they use on-prem DNS via inbound resolver endpoints, and they forward `amazonaws.com` zones, they can break private DNS. Installer should tell them: “check Route53 resolver rules / DHCP options domain-name-servers.”

Hanae Suzuki: +1. We can’t introspect that fully, but we can add a targeted warning when private DNS is enabled and VPC has custom DHCP options set (domain-name-servers not AmazonProvidedDNS).

Victor Alvarez: Nice. That’s detectable.

Nina Petrova: Action items from this thread:
1) Module defaults: endpoints ON, private DNS ON for interface endpoints, S3 gateway for S3
2) Document override for private DNS + hosted zone conflicts
3) Installer preflight: describe endpoints + privateDnsEnabled + VPC DNS flags + NAT detection; fail if missing required endpoints when no NAT
4) Troubleshooting text for DNS resolution + on-prem resolver forwarding pitfalls

Irene Choi: Works for me. Victor can you capture in ADR comment + ping Bruno (module) + Nina (installer) so we’re aligned?

Victor Alvarez: Yep. I’ll summarize + add to the PR notes. :thumbsup:

Sean Gallagher: + please make sure preflight runs before any expensive provisioning steps. We want fast feedback.

Victor Alvarez: Noted. Closing this out unless more concerns.

title: #eng-oncall: 1747684422
preview:
Tomáš Novák: doing a quick dry-run of the KMS decrypt failures runbook in staging before we publish. goal: follow it literally + note anything confusing/missing. staging target: `stg-usw2` control-plane + one data-plane worker pool.

Tomáš Novák: scenario A we’re simulating: intermittent KMS timeouts + some throttles. I’m going to inject failures via the test harness. please drop notes as you go.

PagerDuty Bot: :rotating_light: TEST EVENT: "KMS dependency degraded" triggered in STAGING (service=control-plane, env=stg-usw2). Routed to #eng-oncall.

Sean Gallagher: ok following runbook as-written. first issue: it says “check KMS dashboard” but doesn’t link it. I found it via Observability Pack -> Dependencies -> KMS, but we should paste the actual URL.

Allison Grant: +1. also the dashboard name changed last week (PR-844). it’s now “KMS / Envelope Encryption (platform)” not “KMS health”.

Kira Thompson: noted. I’ll add exact link + update screenshot refs. can someone paste the canonical grafana path?

Allison Grant: try: `https://grafana.redwoodinference.internal/d/deps-kms/kms-envelope-encryption` (staging uses same UID). If not accessible, link to “search: deps-kms”.

Tomáš Novák: injecting now. expect p95 kms latency spikes + some `DeadlineExceeded` in logs.

Caleb Johnson: runbook step 1 says “look for `kms.error_code` in traces”. but in Honeycomb the field is `kms.error.kind` + `kms.provider_code` (per PR-18515). we should align naming.

Kevin Osei: yeah error taxonomy landed as `kms.error.kind` values: `Timeout`, `Throttling`, `AccessDenied`, `NotFound`, `InvalidCiphertext`, `Unknown`. `kms.provider_code` carries AWS/GCP/Azure code.

Kira Thompson: can you both paste the exact field names + example query so I can drop it into the runbook?

Caleb Johnson: traces query example:
`service.name = "control-plane" AND span.name = "kms.decrypt" AND kms.error.kind != ""`
then break down by `kms.error.kind` + `deployment.region`.

Sean Gallagher: runbook step 2 (logs) is a bit vague. it says “check structured logs for decrypt failures” but not the grep. I’m using:
`kubectl -n control-plane logs deploy/api --since=15m | rg 'kms' | rg 'decrypt|envelope'`
But better is to use log explorer with fields.

Allison Grant: agree. preferred: log explorer query by `event="kms_call"` OR `component="kms"` and filter `kms.error.kind`. also we should include the request-id field name (`req_id`) to correlate with 5xx.

Tomáš Novák: here’s an example log line from staging while injection is on:
```
{"level":"warn","msg":"kms decrypt failed","component":"kms","kms.error.kind":"Timeout","kms.provider_code":"DeadlineExceeded","kms.key_arn":"arn:aws:kms:us-west-2:***:key/9c1f...","retry.attempt":2,"retry.remaining_budget_ms":120,"latency_ms":980,"req_id":"01JVE7B7V5G7...","tenant":"internal-staging"}
```

Sean Gallagher: perfect. we should paste that into runbook (redacted) + explain what’s actionable.

Rafael Mendes: following mitigation section: it says “enable degrade mode” but doesn’t say where/what. is that the circuit breaker flag in control-plane config?

Tomáš Novák: yep. it’s the `kms.circuit_breaker.enabled=true` (should already be default) + the “fail-fast after open” behavior is controlled by `kms.circuit_breaker.open_fail_fast=true`. runbook should say how to confirm it’s actually open.

Allison Grant: we have metrics for that: `redwood_kms_circuit_breaker_state{state="open"}` (0/1 gauge). add to runbook.

Kevin Osei: also add the retry budget metric. in the near-miss we saw retry amplification. now we have `redwood_kms_retry_budget_exhausted_total` and `redwood_kms_retry_attempts_total`.

Sean Gallagher: another confusion: runbook says “if timeout spike, reduce retries” but doesn’t give the safe knob + bounds. do we want to reference the guardrails from PR-18502?

Connor O'Brien: +1. for oncall we should provide the exact config path + a warning about overrides. otherwise people will go hunting.

Tomáš Novák: suggestion for runbook text:
- first try: lower `kms.retry.max_attempts` to 1 (staging/prod via config service)
- keep `kms.timeout_ms` at 1000 (don’t raise)
- if we must override guardrails, requires #eng-releases + SRE approval
We should confirm the current prod defaults though.

Allison Grant: defaults are: `timeout_ms=1000`, `max_attempts=2`, exponential backoff w/ jitter, overall cap via `retry_budget_ms=1500`. (from PR-18502 + hotfix PR-18563).

Kira Thompson: ok adding a “Safe default knobs” box + callout for the approval requirement.

Hanae Suzuki: security note: runbook should differentiate “KMS outage/degraded” vs “InvalidCiphertext / key material mismatch”. mitigation is different. right now it lumps them under “decrypt failures”.

Hanae Suzuki: for `InvalidCiphertext`: do NOT suggest “just retry”. it’s usually wrong key ARN, wrong key version, or cutover state mismatch. guidance should include checking cutover flags and dual-read window.

Sean Gallagher: +1. I hit that in the doc: “if invalid ciphertext, rotate keys” is scary. better: “pause cutover, enable dual-read, validate key IDs, escalate to Security + Platform”.

Tomáš Novák: agreed. in this dry run we’re only on timeouts, but we should edit that section now.

Kevin Osei: also for `AccessDenied`: runbook should say to check IAM role ARN and recent terraform changes. most common is missing `kms:Decrypt` on the correct key resource, or using the rotation principal for runtime decrypt (role separation).

Caleb Johnson: traces side: add a note that `AccessDenied` errors are usually fast (low latency) vs timeouts, so on dashboards you’ll see error rate up w/o latency spike. could help differentiate.

Rafael Mendes: I’m at step “verify scope / blast radius”. runbook says “check if impacts hosted vs private” but doesn’t say how. maybe add: look at `deployment.mode` tag in metrics? do we have it?

Allison Grant: we tag metrics with `env` + `region` + `service`. not `deployment.mode` everywhere yet. best short-term: use “tenant” or “cluster” breakdown (private clusters have `cluster_name=priv-*`). add that as heuristic.

Kira Thompson: will add a table:
- Hosted/Dedicated: clusters `core-*`
- Private: clusters `priv-*` (or customer-named)
And point to the “KMS health dashboard -> by cluster” panel.

Tomáš Novák: continuing: I see elevated 5xx in staging API. runbook says “confirm this is KMS-derived 5xx” — we should include the metric query for that.

Sean Gallagher: I used:
`sum(rate(http_server_requests_total{status=~"5..",service="api"}[5m])) by (route)`
then correlate with `sum(rate(redwood_kms_errors_total[5m])) by (kms_error_kind)`.

Allison Grant: metric names in our stack are slightly different (prom):
- `redwood_http_requests_total{status_code=~"5.."}`
- `redwood_kms_errors_total{error_kind="Timeout"}`
Let’s include both prom + grafana panel pointer.

Tomáš Novák: ok, injection off. system recovered in ~3m once throttle stopped. circuit breaker opened briefly but we didn’t page again.

PagerDuty Bot: RESOLVED: TEST EVENT "KMS dependency degraded" in STAGING. Duration: 7m 12s.

Connor O'Brien: runbook “Escalation” section: needs concrete who/where. i.e. tag #eng-security for `InvalidCiphertext`/key mismatch, #eng-infra for IAM/policy, #eng-sre for dependency health. also include “when to open incident” thresholds.

Sean Gallagher: yes. add paging guidance:
- page SRE if `redwood_kms_errors_total` > X for Y mins OR `p95 latency` > threshold
- open incident if customer impact confirmed (prod) or if decrypt failures exceed 0.5% for 10m
(we can tune numbers but need *something*).

Allison Grant: I’ll propose thresholds consistent w/ PR-844 tuning notes: warn at 0.1% decrypt failures, page at 0.5% for 10m (prod only). staging should be non-paging.

Hanae Suzuki: also add explicit “Do not disable envelope encryption” statement. we had someone suggest it during a past incident. the runbook should say it’s not an approved mitigation.

Kira Thompson: adding “Not approved mitigations” section:
- disabling encryption
- widening IAM to `kms:*`
- extending retry budgets without approval

Kevin Osei: can we add an “evidence” note? even for incidents, capture req_ids + sample logs + config changes + who approved. helps later audits.

Tomáš Novák: good call. maybe link the rotation/evidence template but at least say “paste links in incident doc”.

Caleb Johnson: small doc nit: the runbook references `/healthz` but new endpoint is `/_internal/kms/health` (PR-18527). add curl example:
`curl -sS http://localhost:8080/_internal/kms/health | jq`

Sean Gallagher: another nit: needs a pre-req at top: “you need access to config service + grafana + log explorer”. I wasted 2 mins figuring out which kube context.

Kira Thompson: got it. I’ll add a “Prereqs / access” block + kube context examples:
- `kubectx stg-usw2-core`
- `kubens control-plane`

Tomáš Novák: summary of edits from dry-run:
1) Add direct links to KMS dashboard + panels (Allison)
2) Align field names (`kms.error.kind`, `kms.provider_code`, `req_id`) + example queries (Caleb/Kevin)
3) Clarify degrade mode/circuit breaker confirmation + metric (Allison)
4) Add safe knob guidance for retries/timeouts + guardrail note (Sean/Connor)
5) Split guidance by error kind: Timeout/Throttling vs AccessDenied vs InvalidCiphertext (Hanae/Kevin)
6) Add cluster heuristics for hosted vs private impact (Kira)
7) Update healthcheck endpoint path + curl example (Caleb)
8) Add escalation + paging thresholds + “not approved mitigations” + evidence capture note (everyone)

Tomáš Novák: Kira, want me to open a doc PR / confluence edit request or are you making the edits directly?

Kira Thompson: I’ll take first pass edits in Confluence today, then ping back here for final review. If you can paste any missing links (grafana + log explorer) that’d help.

Allison Grant: I’ll DM you the grafana links + the exact metric names from the dashboard JSON.

Sean Gallagher: I’ll add a short section to the runbook about “how to tell if this is a platform-wide KMS issue vs a single tenant/cluster” and send text.

Hanae Suzuki: I’ll draft the `InvalidCiphertext` / cutover / dual-read guidance (incl “pause cutover” steps) and send to Kira.

Tomáš Novák: cool. once updated, we’ll do a second dry run next week (include `AccessDenied` simulation maybe) before publishing widely.

Allison Grant: I’ll DM you the grafana links + the exact metric names from the dashboard JSON.

Sean Gallagher: I’ll add a short section to the runbook about “how to tell if this is a platform-wide KMS issue vs a single tenant/cluster” and send text.

preview:
Bruno Silva: Heads up: Terraform module changes for KMS are ready for review. This is the follow-on to the least-privilege work + rotation role split. Draft PR: https://github.com/redwood-terraform/pull/611 ("add least-privilege KMS IAM modules").

Bruno Silva: TL;DR
- New submodules: `kms_runtime_role` (Decrypt/DataKey) and `kms_rotation_role` (DescribeKey + CreateGrant/PutKeyPolicy bits depending on cloud).
- Default behavior stays close to existing, but we *will* add new outputs and a couple of variables.
- Need to decide version bump + compatibility notes for existing Private customers.

Jordan Lee: looking now. Do we currently have customers pinning the module version in their Private deploy repos? If yes, we need to be careful about a breaking major.

Bruno Silva: yep, most Private customers pin `redwood/private` terraform modules. Some are pinned by installer-generated `versions.tf` too. That’s why I’m trying to avoid a surprise break.

Sean Gallagher: from SRE side: the big concern is rollout sequence. If we ship module changes that tighten IAM and someone applies before the runtime wrapper cutover is fully deployed, we get decrypt failures.

Bruno Silva: +1. In PR I kept the existing policy attachments as defaults, and added a new `enable_separate_rotation_principal` flag (default false) so we don’t force split principals immediately.

Jordan Lee: I see it. Variable name: `separate_rotation_principal_enabled` in one file and `enable_separate_rotation_principal` in README. Can we standardize? :)

Bruno Silva: good catch, will fix.

Marcus Lin: Security POV: we should still message this as “recommended split roles” and call out that rotation actions should not run under the runtime decrypt principal. But I’m ok with defaulting to current behavior for compatibility.

Allison Grant: also please make sure module exports the role ARN consistently. Observability hooks expect the label `kms_principal` to match what we document (role ARN string).

Bruno Silva: module outputs right now:
- existing: `kms_role_arn`
- new: `kms_runtime_role_arn`, `kms_rotation_role_arn`
I left `kms_role_arn` as an alias to runtime for now.

Jordan Lee: that alias helps. Please document that `kms_role_arn` is deprecated but still present.

Vivek Kulkarni: for Private: installer will generate terraform config. We can update installer templates to use the new outputs, but we need a path for existing customers who don’t upgrade installer immediately.

Bruno Silva: exactly. Proposed compatibility plan:
1) Terraform module release: minor bump (e.g. 3.6.0 -> 3.7.0) w/ new roles behind flag, keep old outputs.
2) Installer update references new outputs but continues to accept old variables if present.
3) After 1-2 quarters, we can do a major bump that removes deprecated outputs.

Connor O'Brien: release process question: do we publish these modules with semver tags? if so, we should treat output removals as major.

Bruno Silva: yes, internal semver tags. That’s why I’m leaning minor now.

Sean Gallagher: can we also add a “safety check” note: apply in staging first; verify decrypt calls before tightening? People will copy/paste.

Bruno Silva: yep. Adding README section: “Upgrade notes (Private existing customers)” with a checklist:
- Ensure KMS wrapper + circuit breaker changes deployed (>= runtime build X)
- Apply terraform
- Confirm dashboards: KMS AccessDenied=0, latency stable
- Then enable `enable_separate_rotation_principal=true` in a follow-up change

Jordan Lee: please be explicit about permissions.
Runtime principal should need:
- `kms:Decrypt`
- `kms:GenerateDataKey` (or `GenerateDataKeyWithoutPlaintext` depending)
Rotation principal should need:
- `kms:DescribeKey`
- and whatever is used to update alias/key versioning from our control plane (we generally don’t want `kms:PutKeyPolicy` unless customer model requires it)

Marcus Lin: +1. Also mention: customers may have SCPs / org policies; we should avoid adding optional permissions by default.

Bruno Silva: PR currently uses a conservative base policy and exposes `extra_kms_actions_runtime` / `extra_kms_actions_rotation` lists if needed.

Allison Grant: any renames to metric tags needed? If the role changes, we should ensure dashboards don’t break. But that’s probably on the app side.

Sean Gallagher: oncall pain point: customers apply Terraform and then file a ticket with an AccessDenied. If we can add a note: “common failure mode: key policy doesn’t allow new role ARN” that’d save cycles.

Bruno Silva: adding to README troubleshooting:
- “AccessDenied after applying: update key policy to trust the runtime role ARN; if enabling split roles, ensure both ARNs are trusted during transition.”

Vivek Kulkarni: can we align this with installer preflight validation? The installer PR adds checks (I think PR-392). If terraform changes cause new role ARN, preflight should warn if key policy doesn’t include it.

Bruno Silva: yep, I’ll sync w/ that. https://github.com/redwood-private-installer/pull/392 is the one.

Jordan Lee: one more: module currently changes the default session name / role path? Some customers rely on predictable IAM paths.

Bruno Silva: no role path change. Names will be stable unless `name_prefix` differs. I did add suffixes `-runtime` and `-rotation` when split enabled.

Connor O'Brien: what’s the plan for existing customers who already have a single role and don’t want terraform to replace it? Will enabling split force replacement?

Bruno Silva: enabling split creates *additional* role resources, doesn’t destroy existing. If they keep split disabled, resources are unchanged.

Sean Gallagher: good. Also please avoid “force new” on the role by changing assume role policy unexpectedly.

Bruno Silva: no assume role policy changes when split disabled.

Marcus Lin: version bump proposal sounds right: minor bump now with deprecation warnings.

Jordan Lee: can you add a changelog entry that explicitly says “no breaking changes if you don’t enable split”? People skim.

Bruno Silva: will do.

Allison Grant: if we can get a single canonical snippet for Private upgrade docs, I can link it from the KMS dashboards page too.

Bruno Silva: I’ll paste draft snippet here once README updates are in.

Sean Gallagher: request: include a note about staged rollout: enable split roles only after verifying dual-read / rotation window logic if they’re doing key rotation same week.

Bruno Silva: added note: “Don’t combine IAM tightening + key rotation cutover in the same maintenance window unless you have rollback.”

Vivek Kulkarni: :thumbsup: also note some customers do quarterly rotations w/ audit evidence; they’ll ask if this changes their rotation procedure.

Marcus Lin: we can answer: “no change required; split roles is recommended hardening; rotation procedure is the same, just uses different principal.”

Bruno Silva: ok, action items from this thread:
- Fix variable naming consistency
- Add README: upgrade notes, troubleshooting, staged rollout checklist
- Add changelog entry for minor bump
- Confirm no unexpected role replacement
I’ll push updates and re-request review on PR-611 today.

Jordan Lee: thanks. I’ll do detailed policy review once updated.

Sean Gallagher: I’ll skim for rollout hazards / defaults.

Connor O'Brien: once merged, ping me and I’ll coordinate tagging + internal announcement so Private folks don’t get surprised.

Bruno Silva: will do.
